[Brad Pitt](/wiki/Brad_Pitt "Brad Pitt") is an American actor and film producer. His acting career began at age 23 in 1987 with roles in the hit Fox television series *[21 Jump Street](/wiki/21_Jump_Street "21 Jump Street")*. He subsequently appeared in episodes for television shows during the late 1980s and played his first major role in the slasher film *[Cutting Class](/wiki/Cutting_Class "Cutting Class")* (1989).[[1]](#cite_note-1) He gained recognition in *[Thelma & Louise](/wiki/Thelma_%26_Louise "Thelma & Louise")* (1991), *[A River Runs Through It](/wiki/A_River_Runs_Through_It_(film) "A River Runs Through It (film)")* (1992), *[Cool World](/wiki/Cool_World "Cool World")* (1992) and *[Kalifornia](/wiki/Kalifornia "Kalifornia")* (1993).[[2]](#cite_note-Thelma-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)
He later took on the role of [vampire](/wiki/Vampire "Vampire") [Louis de Pointe du Lac](/wiki/Louis_de_Pointe_du_Lac "Louis de Pointe du Lac") in the horror drama *[Interview with the Vampire](/wiki/Interview_with_the_Vampire_(film) "Interview with the Vampire (film)")* (1994) and for his performance in the epic drama *[Legends of the Fall](/wiki/Legends_of_the_Fall "Legends of the Fall")* (1994), he earned his first [Golden Globe Award for Best Actor](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Actor_%E2%80%93_Motion_Picture_Drama "Golden Globe Award for Best Actor – Motion Picture Drama") nomination.[[4]](#cite_note-Achath2011-4)
Pitt starred in the [David Fincher](/wiki/David_Fincher "David Fincher")-directed, commercially successful thriller *[Seven](/wiki/Seven_(1995_film) "Seven (1995 film)")* (1995), in which he played a detective on the trail of a [serial killer](/wiki/Serial_killer "Serial killer") who murders people he believes are guilty of the [seven deadly sins](/wiki/Seven_deadly_sins "Seven deadly sins").[[5]](#cite_note-WinterHughes2007-5) His performance as a psychotic mental patient in the science fiction film *[12 Monkeys](/wiki/12_Monkeys "12 Monkeys")* won him the [Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actor](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor_%E2%80%93_Motion_Picture "Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actor – Motion Picture") and an Academy Award nomination in [the same category](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor").[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) He followed it with the role of [Heinrich Harrer](/wiki/Heinrich_Harrer "Heinrich Harrer") in the biopic *[Seven Years in Tibet](/wiki/Seven_Years_in_Tibet_(1997_film) "Seven Years in Tibet (1997 film)")* (1997) and as [Death](/wiki/Personifications_of_death "Personifications of death") in *[Meet Joe Black](/wiki/Meet_Joe_Black "Meet Joe Black")* (1998).[[8]](#cite_note-8) Pitt reteamed with Fincher to star in the apocalyptic film *[Fight Club](/wiki/Fight_Club "Fight Club")* (1999) as anti-consumerist cult leader [Tyler Durden](/wiki/Tyler_Durden "Tyler Durden"), a role that required him to learn [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), [taekwondo](/wiki/Taekwondo "Taekwondo"), and [grappling](/wiki/Grappling "Grappling").[[9]](#cite_note-9) A critical and commercial disappointment at the time of release, the film has since developed a [cult status](/wiki/Cult_following "Cult following").[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) Pitt portrayed [Rusty Ryan](/wiki/Rusty_Ryan_(character) "Rusty Ryan (character)") in the commercially successful heist film series *[Ocean's Trilogy](/wiki/Ocean%27s_Trilogy "Ocean's Trilogy")* (2001–07).[[12]](#cite_note-12) In 2002, he earned a [Primetime Emmy Award nomination](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Guest_Actor_in_a_Comedy_Series "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Guest Actor in a Comedy Series") for his guest appearance in the sitcom *[Friends](/wiki/Friends_(TV_series) "Friends (TV series)")* alongside his then-wife [Jennifer Aniston](/wiki/Jennifer_Aniston "Jennifer Aniston").[[13]](#cite_note-13) Also that year, Pitt started a production company, [Plan B Entertainment](/wiki/Plan_B_Entertainment "Plan B Entertainment"),[[14]](#cite_note-14) whose first release was the epic war film *[Troy](/wiki/Troy_(film) "Troy (film)")* (2004), starring Pitt. He played an assassin opposite [Angelina Jolie](/wiki/Angelina_Jolie "Angelina Jolie") in the commercially successful action comedy *[Mr. & Mrs. Smith](/wiki/Mr._%26_Mrs._Smith_(2005_film) "Mr. & Mrs. Smith (2005 film)")* (2005).[[15]](#cite_note-15)
In 2006, Pitt produced the crime drama *[The Departed](/wiki/The_Departed "The Departed")* and starred alongside [Cate Blanchett](/wiki/Cate_Blanchett "Cate Blanchett") in the [multi-narrative](/wiki/Multiperspectivity "Multiperspectivity") drama *[Babel](/wiki/Babel_(film) "Babel (film)")*; the former won the [Academy Award for Best Picture](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Picture "Academy Award for Best Picture"). Pitt's portrayal of the eponymous man who ages in reverse in the drama *[The Curious Case of Benjamin Button](/wiki/The_Curious_Case_of_Benjamin_Button_(film) "The Curious Case of Benjamin Button (film)")* (2008) earned him an [Academy Award for Best Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actor "Academy Award for Best Actor") nomination.[[16]](#cite_note-16) He starred in the successful war film *[Inglourious Basterds](/wiki/Inglourious_Basterds "Inglourious Basterds")* (2009),[[17]](#cite_note-17) and produced the superhero film *[Kick-Ass](/wiki/Kick-Ass_(film) "Kick-Ass (film)")* (2010) and [its sequel](/wiki/Kick-Ass_2_(film) "Kick-Ass 2 (film)") in 2013. In 2011, he earned critical acclaim for producing and starring in two films—the experimental drama *[The Tree of Life](/wiki/The_Tree_of_Life_(film) "The Tree of Life (film)")* and the biopic sports drama *[Moneyball](/wiki/Moneyball_(film) "Moneyball (film)")*—both of which were nominated for the Academy Award for Best Picture.[[18]](#cite_note-18) He also garnered a Best Actor nomination for the latter. His biggest commercial success as a leading actor for many years was the apocalyptic film *[World War Z](/wiki/World_War_Z_(film) "World War Z (film)")* (2013), which grossed $540 million worldwide.[[19]](#cite_note-19) That was only eclipsed by the [Apple Studios](/wiki/Apple_Studios "Apple Studios") produced film [F1](/wiki/F1_(film) "F1 (film)") in 2025. Pitt produced the period drama *[12 Years a Slave](/wiki/12_Years_a_Slave_(film) "12 Years a Slave (film)")* (2013), for which he won the Academy Award for Best Picture.[[20]](#cite_note-20) In 2014, he starred in the war film *[Fury](/wiki/Fury_(2014_film) "Fury (2014 film)")* which received positive reviews from critics and proved to be successful at the box office.[[21]](#cite_note-21) For playing a stunt double to [Leonardo DiCaprio](/wiki/Leonardo_DiCaprio "Leonardo DiCaprio")'s character in [Quentin Tarantino](/wiki/Quentin_Tarantino "Quentin Tarantino")'s film *[Once Upon a Time in Hollywood](/wiki/Once_Upon_a_Time_in_Hollywood "Once Upon a Time in Hollywood")* (2019), he won an [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor"), a [Golden Globe Award](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor_%E2%80%93_Motion_Picture "Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actor – Motion Picture"), and a [BAFTA Award for Best Actor in a Supporting Role](/wiki/BAFTA_Award_for_Best_Actor_in_a_Supporting_Role "BAFTA Award for Best Actor in a Supporting Role").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

This **list of U.S. states and territories by educational attainment** covers the 50 [U.S.](/wiki/United_States "United States") [states](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state"), the [District of Columbia](/wiki/District_of_Columbia "District of Columbia"), and the [territory](/wiki/Territories_of_the_United_States "Territories of the United States") of [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico") and their populations' [educational attainment](/wiki/Educational_attainment "Educational attainment") for all people of at least 25 years of age. The four other inhabited U.S. territories ([American Samoa](/wiki/American_Samoa "American Samoa"), [Guam](/wiki/Guam "Guam"), the [Northern Mariana Islands](/wiki/Northern_Mariana_Islands "Northern Mariana Islands"), and the [U.S. Virgin Islands](/wiki/U.S._Virgin_Islands "U.S. Virgin Islands")) are listed separately.
The data source for the main list is the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/U.S._Census_Bureau "U.S. Census Bureau")'s [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") in 2021.[[1]](#cite_note-CensusData-1) The data source for the territories table is from 2010 U.S. Census Bureau data, because those territories are excluded from the American Community Survey.[[2]](#cite_note-Guam-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Virgin_Islands-3) The American Community Survey is a large demographic survey collected throughout the year using mailed questionnaires, telephone interviews, and visits from Census Bureau field representatives to about 3.5 million household addresses annually, regardless of their legal immigration status. The survey does not measure graduation rates from different educational institutions, but instead, it measures the percentage of adult residents with a high school diploma.[[4]](#cite_note-4)
Overall, 90.3% of Americans over the age of 25 had graduated from high school in 2021, with the highest level found in the state of [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") at 96.1% and the lowest in the state of [California](/wiki/California "California") at 84.4%. In [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico"), the proportion was even lower, though, at 79.6%.[[1]](#cite_note-CensusData-1)
34.9% of Americans over the age of 25 had educational attainment of having a [bachelor's degree](/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree "Bachelor's degree") or higher in 2019. question: What was the average 911 call answer time in Washington D.C. on April 30, 2025?
As of 2022, how many Indian states had any political party win more than 50% of the vote in assembly elections exactly twice?

sources.snippet:
more than 50 % of the vote share****
No individual party has ever secured more than [50% vote in a Parliamentary Election,](https://eci.gov.in/statistical-report/statistical-reports/) but in assembly elections, an individual party secured more than 50% of the vote on 42 occasions (including Gujarat 2022).
In 1951, Indian National Congress (INC) managed to secure more than half of the votes polled across 4 states. This includes Sourashtra, Coorg , Bhopal (the three no longer exist as states) & Delhi.
The current result in Gujarat, is not the first time in the state where a party polled more than 50%. This was the fifth time that a political party has received a decisive mandate by securing more than 50% of the vote share. While, the 156 seats won by BJP is the highest in state’s history, the vote share is not the highest. INC secured 55.5% of the vote share in 1985 state assembly elections.
As highlighted earlier, it was 7 years ago that another political party managed to secure more than 50% in Assembly elections i.e. AAP in Delhi.
However, one has to go back to much farther i.e., 1990, for a political party to secure more than 50% of votes share in a large state. Janata Dal secured 53.7% of votes in Odisha Assembly elections in 1990. Post that, this happened only in the smaller North-Eastern states.
**It happened most times in Sikkim**
Out of the 42 times that a political party secured more than 50% of vote share, the most times it happened is in Sikkim (6 times). In 1985 Assembly elections, Sikkim Sangram Parishad secured 62.2% of the vote share, the first time in the state. This was repeated in the next elections. After a break, in four successive assembly elections i.e., 1999, 2004, 2009 & 2014, the Sikkim Democratic Front secure more than 50% of vote share.
This feat of a political party securing more than 50% vote share happened in 18 different states. Gujarat has the second highest number of such instances with 5 and also the highest among the large states.
Arunachal Pradesh, Assam, Jammu & Kashmir (when it was a state) & Karnataka have 3 such instances each.
****Which Political party achieved this feat the most number of times?****
The 2022 Gujarat elections were the first time that BJP has managed to secure more than 50% of the votes polled in a state assembly election contesting alone. Overall 9 different political parties have achieved this feat.
The Indian National Congress did this the highest number of times with 30. The most recent was in 2009, in Arunachal Pradesh, where the INC secured 50.4% of the vote share.
If the large states are considered, the last time INC secured more than 50% of the vote share was in 1985. It secured 55.5% in Gujarat and 51.08% in Odisha.
Only two other political parties have managed to do this more than once, both of them belonging to Sikkim. The Sikkim Democratic Front won consecutively for four terms – 1999, 2004, 2009 & 2014. Prior to that, the Sikkim Sangram Parishad did in two consecutive terms – 1985, 1989.
**Such occurrences have become rarer in the recent decades**
Out of the 42 times that a party has secured more than 50% vote share, 19 have been during the decades 1971-80 and 1981-90. During the first decade of the Indian republic, this happened in 6 assembly elections, all by INC. In the next three decades it was 7, 10 & 9 respectively.
The 90s were a decade of political instability and during that period, this occurred only in 4 Assembly elections, all in North-Eastern states. The occurrences became much rarer, with only 6 such instances since the  turn of the century.
Various reasons can be cited for the emergence of this trend. Unlike the earlier decades, there is no single party dominance like in case of INC that dominated the elections. The emergence of strong regional & sub-regional parties & national alternatives, alliances among parties, etc. have contributed to a fractured vote share. Apart from AAP’s victory in 2015, BJP’s recent performance in Gujarat is significant in this context.
****SDF’s performance in 2004 Sikkim elections is the best ever****
As already highlighted above, two regional parties from Sikkim i.e., Sikkim Sangram Parishad and Sikkim Democratic Front are the only ones that have managed to repeat this feat more than once apart from the INC. They also occupy 3 out of top-5 positions in terms of vote share secured by a party.
In 2004 Sikkim Assembly elections, Sikkim Democratic Party retained power in the state on the back of polling 71.09% of the vote share. This bettered the performance of Sikkim Sangram Parishad in 1989 assembly elections of 70.41%.
Among the large states, National Conference in the erst-while state of Jammu & Kashmir secured 66.96% of the vote share in 1962 elections.
The best performance of Indian National Congress in terms of vote share was in the erst while state of Saurashtra in 1951 with 63.79% of vote share.
****A political party has managed to win all the seats only twice****
In the recent Gujarat assembly elections of 2022, BJP has managed to win 156 out of 182 seats, which is around 85.7% of the total seats. Earlier in 2015, AAP which secured 54.3 % of the votes has managed to win 67 out of 70 assembly seats in Delhi.
Only on two occasions in the last has the winning party secured more than 50% of the votes and has won all the seats. In 1989 Sikkim Assembly elections, the Sikkim Sangram Parishad has won all the 32 seats and secured a vote share of 70.41%. In 2009, Sikkim Democratic Front has repeated the feat with 65.9% vote share winning all 32 seats on offer.
India follows a first-past-the-post system, which often means that the number of seats won is not necessarily proportionate to the vote share secured.

   Aditya Paul on  [November 10, 2016 11:31 am](https://factly.in/many-times-political-party-get-50-votes-2/#comment-8211 "November 10, 2016 at 11:31 am")
   Hi Shashi,  
    Thanks for this very informative article..
   I’m looking for data on what has been the vote share of national level parties \*when their head has been the PM\*.. like when Indira Gandhi was PM, what ℅ of people voted for Cong (I), Deve Gowda, Narender Modi.. etc..
   Can you guide me please..
3. ![](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/fbcd99d6583a0c41fea2848f3c6bf8c4?s=40&d=mm&r=g)
   Abdul Momin on  [December 6, 2017 9:54 pm](https://factly.in/many-times-political-party-get-50-votes-2/#comment-8212 "December 6, 2017 at 9:54 pm")
   You need to give the number of seats won by this percentage & whether these were fought between multiple parties or 2 opponents ,
   AAP got 54% which gave them 95% of the seats.  
    In the coming elections we will be having moslty multi party election which means this single party percentage wont matter much.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/First_World_Hotel_Genting_%2B_Theme_park.jpg/250px-First_World_Hotel_Genting_%2B_Theme_park.jpg)](/wiki/File:First_World_Hotel_Genting_%2B_Theme_park.jpg)
[First World Hotel](/wiki/First_World_Hotel "First World Hotel") in [Genting Highlands, Pahang](/wiki/Genting_Highlands,_Pahang "Genting Highlands, Pahang"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") is the largest hotel in the world.
This is a list of [hotels](/wiki/Hotel "Hotel") that have 3,000 or more guest accommodation rooms. Since 2020, the largest hotel in the world is the [First World Hotel](/wiki/First_World_Hotel "First World Hotel") in [Pahang](/wiki/Pahang "Pahang"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") with 6,119 rooms divided between three buildings.[[1]](#cite_note-Guinness2015-1) The largest single hotel building is the [MGM Grand Las Vegas](/wiki/MGM_Grand_Las_Vegas "MGM Grand Las Vegas"), with 5,044 rooms in one building.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-FV-3)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Below is a list of notable **[private equity firms](/wiki/Private_equity_firm "Private equity firm")**.
Largest private equity firms by PE capital raised
-------------------------------------------------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_private_equity_firms&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Largest private equity firms by PE capital raised")]
Each year [Private Equity International](/wiki/Private_Equity_International "Private Equity International") publishes the PEI 300, a ranking of the largest private-equity firms by how much capital they have raised for private-equity investment in the last five years.[[1]](#cite_note-1)
In the 2025 ranking, [KKR](/wiki/KKR_%26_Co. "KKR & Co.") regained the top spot from [Blackstone Inc.](/wiki/Blackstone_Inc. "Blackstone Inc.") who fell to third, behind [EQT AB](/wiki/EQT_AB "EQT AB").[[2]](#cite_note-2)@media screen{.mw-parser-output .sort-under.sortable.wikitable th.headerSort,.mw-parser-output question: If I randomly select i.i.d. samples from a distribution n times, the distribution of the sampled variables will approximately follow a normal distribution as n becomes very large. This statement is based on which theory?
answer: There is no theory that supports this statement.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Fundamental theorem in probability theory and statistics
.mw-parser-output .infobox-subbox{padding:0;border:none;margin:-3px;width:auto;min-width:100%;font-size:100%;clear:none;float:none;background-color:transparent;color:inherit}.mw-parser-output .infobox-3cols-child{margin:-3px}.mw-parser-output .infobox .navbar{font-size:100%}@media screen{html.skin-theme-clientpref-night .mw-parser-output .infobox-full-data:not(.notheme)>div:not(.notheme)[style]{background:#1f1f23!important;color:#f8f9fa}}@media screen and (prefers-color-scheme:dark){html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .infobox-full-data:not(.notheme)>div:not(.notheme)[style]{background:#1f1f23!important;color:#f8f9fa}}@media(min-width:640px){body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table{display:table!important}body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table>caption{display:table-caption!important}body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table>tbody{display:table-row-group}body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table th,body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table td{padding-left:inherit;padding-right:inherit}}
Central Limit Theorem
|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Type | [Theorem](/wiki/Theorem "Theorem") |
| Field | [Probability theory](/wiki/Probability_theory "Probability theory") |
| Statement | The scaled sum of a sequence of [i.i.d. random variables](/wiki/Independent_and_identically_distributed_random_variables "Independent and identically distributed random variables") with finite positive [variance](/wiki/Variance "Variance") converges in distribution to the [normal distribution](/wiki/Normal_distribution "Normal distribution"). |
| Generalizations | [Lindeberg's CLT](/wiki/Lindeberg%27s_condition "Lindeberg's condition") |
In [probability theory](/wiki/Probability_theory "Probability theory"), the **central limit theorem** (**CLT**) states that, under appropriate conditions, the [distribution](/wiki/Probability_distribution "Probability distribution") of a normalized version of the sample mean converges to a [standard normal distribution](/wiki/Normal_distribution#Standard_normal_distribution "Normal distribution"). This holds even if the original variables themselves are not [normally distributed](/wiki/Normal_distribution "Normal distribution"). There are several versions of the CLT, each applying in the context of different conditions.
The theorem is a key concept in probability theory because it implies that probabilistic and statistical methods that work for normal distributions can be applicable to many problems involving other types of distributions.
This theorem has seen many changes during the formal development of probability theory. Previous versions of the theorem date back to 1811, but in its modern form it was only precisely stated in the 1920s.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFischer2011[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_July_2023]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (July_2023)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-1)

![{\displaystyle \sigma ^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/53a5c55e536acf250c1d3e0f754be5692b843ef5).[[2]](#cite_note-2)
In other words, suppose that a large sample of [observations](/wiki/Random_variate "Random variate") is obtained, each observation being randomly produced in a way that does not depend on the values of the other observations, and the average ([arithmetic mean](/wiki/Arithmetic_mean "Arithmetic mean")) of the observed values is computed. If this procedure is performed many times, resulting in a collection of observed averages, the central limit theorem says that if the sample size is large enough, the [probability distribution](/wiki/Probability_distribution "Probability distribution") of these averages will closely approximate a normal distribution.
The central limit theorem has several variants. In its common form, the random variables must be [independent and identically distributed](/wiki/Independent_and_identically_distributed "Independent and identically distributed") (i.i.d.). This requirement can be weakened; convergence of the mean to the normal distribution also occurs for non-identical distributions or for non-independent observations if they comply with certain conditions.
The earliest version of this theorem, that the normal distribution may be used as an approximation to the [binomial distribution](/wiki/Binomial_distribution "Binomial distribution"), is the [de Moivre–Laplace theorem](/wiki/De_Moivre%E2%80%93Laplace_theorem "De Moivre–Laplace theorem").
Independent sequences
---------------------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_limit_theorem&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Independent sequences")]
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/IllustrationCentralTheorem.png/500px-IllustrationCentralTheorem.png)](/wiki/File:IllustrationCentralTheorem.png)
Whatever the form of the population distribution, the sampling distribution tends to a Gaussian, and its dispersion is given by the central limit theorem.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/InternationalOlympiadInInformatics.png/250px-InternationalOlympiadInInformatics.png)](/wiki/File:InternationalOlympiadInInformatics.png)
The logo of the International Olympiad in Informatics
The **International Olympiad in Informatics** (**IOI**) is an annual [competitive programming](/wiki/Competitive_programming "Competitive programming") competition and one of the [International Science Olympiads](/wiki/International_Science_Olympiads "International Science Olympiads") for [secondary school](/wiki/Secondary_school "Secondary school") students. The first IOI was held in 1989 in [Pravetz](/wiki/Pravetz "Pravetz"), Bulgaria.
Each country sends a team of up to four students, plus one team leader, one deputy leader, and guests. Students in each country are selected for their country's team through [national computing contests](#Feeder_competitions). Students at the IOI compete on an individual basis. There is no official team ranking.
The contest consists of two days of solving six complicated [algorithmic](/wiki/Algorithm "Algorithm") tasks by writing computer programs in [C++](/wiki/C%2B%2B "C++").[[1]](#cite_note-Robson-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) All task materials are published on each year's contest website soon after the competition ends.
Competition structure and participation
---------------------------------------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Olympiad_in_Informatics&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Competition structure and participation")]

Back
A bronze medal from IOI 2006 in Mexico
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/IOI_Zagreb_1.JPG/250px-IOI_Zagreb_1.JPG)](/wiki/File:IOI_Zagreb_1.JPG)
In front of the competition room at the IOI 2007
On each of the two competition days, the competitors are typically given three problems which they have to solve in five hours. Each student works on their own to solve the problems with no outside help, specifically no communication with other contestants, books, web access, etc. Contestants are typically allowed to bring non-programmable wired keyboards and mice.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Usually to solve a task the contestant has to write a computer program (in [C++](/wiki/C%2B%2B "C++")) and submit it before the five-hour competition time ends. The program is graded based on secret test data. Since IOI 2010, tasks are divided into subtasks with graduated difficulty, and points are awarded only when all tests for a particular subtask yield correct results, within specific time and memory limits. In some cases, the contestant's program has to interact with a secret computer library, which allows problems where the input is not fixed, but depends on the program's actions – for example in game problems (a.k.a. interactive problems). Another type of problem has the inputs publicly available, for these, the contestants have to submit an output file instead of a program, and it is up to them whether they obtain the output files by writing a program (possibly exploiting special characteristics of the input), or by hand, or by a combination of these means. Pascal has been removed as an available programming language as of 2019.[[4]](#cite_note-minutes16-4):11
IOI 2010 for the first time had a live web scoreboard with real-time provisional results. Submissions will be scored as soon as possible during the contest, and the results posted. Contestants will be aware of their scores, but not others', and may resubmit to improve their scores. Starting from 2012, IOI has been using the Contest Management System (CMS) for developing and monitoring the contest.[[5]](#cite_note-5)
The scores from the two competition days and all problems are summed up separately for each contestant. Medals are awarded depending on their relative total score. The top 50% of the contestants are awarded medals, such that the relative number of gold : silver : bronze : no medal is approximately 1:2:3:6 (thus 1/12 of the contestants get a gold medal).
Prior to IOI 2010, students who did not receive medals did not have their scores published, although the scores of students who did not receive medals are still not available in the official results, they are known from the live web scoreboard. In IOI 2012 the top 3 nations ranked by aggregate score (Russia, China and USA) were subsequently awarded during the closing ceremony.
Analysis of female performance shows 77.9% of women obtain no medal, while 49.2% of men obtain no medal. "The average female participation was 4.4% in 1989–1994 and 2.2% in 1996–2014." It also suggests much higher participation of women on the national level, claiming sometimes double-digit percentages in total participation on the first stage.[[6]](#cite_note-6) President of the IOI (2011-2014), Richard Forster, says the competition has difficulty attracting women and that in spite of trying to solve it, "none of us have hit on quite what the problem is, let alone the solution."[[1]](#cite_note-Robson-1) The [European Girls’ Olympiad in Informatics](/w/index.php?title=European_Girls%E2%80%99_Olympiad_in_Informatics&action=edi

Notable participants
--------------------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_International_Mathematical_Olympiad_participants&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Notable participants")]
### Mathematicians
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_International_Mathematical_Olympiad_participants&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Mathematicians")]
A number of IMO participants have gone on to become notable [mathematicians](/wiki/Mathematician "Mathematician"). The following IMO participants have either received a [Fields Medal](/wiki/Fields_Medal "Fields Medal"), an [Abel Prize](/wiki/Abel_Prize "Abel Prize"), a [Wolf Prize](/wiki/Wolf_Prize_in_Mathematics "Wolf Prize in Mathematics") or a [Clay Research Award](/wiki/Clay_Research_Award "Clay Research Award"), awards which recognise groundbreaking research in mathematics; a [European Mathematical Society Prize](/wiki/European_Mathematical_Society "European Mathematical Society"), an award which recognizes young researchers; or one of the [American Mathematical Society](/wiki/American_Mathematical_Society "American Mathematical Society")'s awards (a [Blumenthal Award](/wiki/Blumenthal_Award "Blumenthal Award") in Pure Mathematics, [Bôcher Memorial Prize](/wiki/B%C3%B4cher_Memorial_Prize "Bôcher Memorial Prize") in Analysis, [Cole Prize in Algebra](/wiki/Cole_Prize "Cole Prize"), [Cole Prize in Number Theory](/wiki/Cole_Prize "Cole Prize"), [Fulkerson Prize in Discrete Mathematics](/wiki/Fulkerson_Prize "Fulkerson Prize"), [Steele Prize in Mathematics](/wiki/Steele_Prize "Steele Prize"), or [Veblen Prize in Geometry and Topology](/wiki/Veblen_Prize "Veblen Prize")) recognizing research in specific mathematical fields. [Grigori Perelman](/wiki/Grigori_Perelman "Grigori Perelman") proved the [Poincaré conjecture](/wiki/Poincar%C3%A9_conjecture "Poincaré conjecture") (one of the seven [Millennium Prize Problems](/wiki/Millennium_Prize_Problems "Millennium Prize Problems")), and [Yuri Matiyasevich](/wiki/Yuri_Matiyasevich "Yuri Matiyasevich") gave a negative solution of [Hilbert's tenth problem](/wiki/Hilbert%27s_tenth_problem "Hilbert's tenth problem").
**G** denotes an IMO gold medal, **S** denotes a silver medal, **B** denotes a bronze medal, and **P** denotes a perfect score.

### Computer scientists
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_International_Mathematical_Olympiad_participants&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Computer scientists")]
IMO medalists have also gone on to become notable [computer scientists](/wiki/Computer_scientists "Computer scientists"). The following IMO medalists have received a [IMU Abacus Medal](/wiki/IMU_Abacus_Medal "IMU Abacus Medal") (earlier known as Nevanlinna Prize), a [Knuth Prize](/wiki/Knuth_Prize "Knuth Prize"), or a [Gödel Prize](/wiki/G%C3%B6del_Prize "Gödel Prize"); these awards recognise research in theoretical computer science. **G** denotes an IMO gold medal, **S** denotes a silver medal, **B** denotes a bronze medal, and **P** denotes a perfect score.
| Name | Team | IMO | [IMU Abacus Medal](/wiki/IMU_Abacus_Medal "IMU Abacus Medal") | [Knuth Prize](/wiki/Knuth_Prize "Knuth Prize") | [Gödel Prize](/wiki/G%C3%B6del_Prize "Gödel Prize") |

| [László Babai](/wiki/L%C3%A1szl%C3%B3_Babai "László Babai") | [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") | **P** 1968, **S** 1967, **S** 1966 |  | **2015** | **1993** |
| [Johan Håstad](/wiki/Johan_H%C3%A5stad "Johan Håstad") | [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") | **G** 1977 |  |  | **1994, 2011** |

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
(Redirected from [List of most-followed Twitter accounts](/w/index.php?title=List_of_most-followed_Twitter_accounts&redirect=no "List of most-followed Twitter accounts"))
.mw-parser-output .hatnote{font-style:italic}.mw-parser-output div.hatnote{padding-left:1.6em;margin-bottom:0.5em}.mw-parser-output .hatnote i{font-style:normal}.mw-parser-output .hatnote+link+.hatnote{margin-top:-0.5em}@media print{body.ns-0 .mw-parser-output .hatnote{display:none!important}}
This is a [dynamic list](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Lists#Dynamic_lists "Wikipedia:WikiProject Lists") and may never be able to satisfy particular standards for completeness. You can help by [editing the page](/wiki/Special:EditPage/List_of_most-followed_X_accounts "Special:EditPage/List of most-followed X accounts") to add missing items, with references to [reliable sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources").
This list contains the top 50 accounts with the most followers on the social media platform [X, formerly and commonly known as Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter"). As of March 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=List_of_most-followed_X_accounts&action=edit), [Elon Musk](/wiki/Elon_Musk "Elon Musk"), the owner of the website, has the most-followed account with over 236 million followers. He partially acquired these followers through changing the code of the platform to promote his own posts more favorably.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)
Other notable figures such as Presidents [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") and [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"), Prime Minister [Narendra Modi](/wiki/Narendra_Modi "Narendra Modi") and [Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo") each have over 100 million followers. [Katy Perry](/wiki/Katy_Perry "Katy Perry") was the first person to surpass the 100 million follower milestone, in June 2017.[[3]](#cite_note-3)
List
----
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_most-followed_X_accounts&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: List")]
The following table lists the top 50 most-followed accounts on X, with each total rounded down to the nearest hundred thousand, as well as a profession of each account's holder.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*] Some accounts post infrequently or are abandoned.
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Elon_Musk_Royal_Society_%28crop2%29.jpg/250px-Elon_Musk_Royal_Society_%28crop2%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Elon_Musk_Royal_Society_(crop2).jpg)
[Elon Musk](/wiki/Elon_Musk "Elon Musk") is the owner of X (formerly Twitter) and the most-followed person on X.
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/President_Barack_Obama.jpg/250px-President_Barack_Obama.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Barack_Obama.jpg)
Former U.S. President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") is the most-followed politician on X.
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Shri_Narendra_Modi%2C_Prime_Minister_of_India_%283x4_cropped%29.jpg/250px-Shri_Narendra_Modi%2C_Prime_Minister_of_India_%283x4_cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shri_Narendra_Modi,_Prime_Minister_of_India_(3x4_cropped).jpg)
Indian Prime Minister [Narendra Modi](/wiki/Narendra_Modi "Narendra Modi") is the most-followed Asian on X.
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Cristiano_Ronaldo_2018.jpg/250px-Cristiano_Ronaldo_2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cristiano_Ronaldo_2018.jpg)
Portuguese footballer [Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo") is the most-followed sports personality on X.
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Rihanna_Fenty_2018.png/250px-Rihanna_Fenty_2018.png)](/wiki/File:Rihanna_Fenty_2018.png)
Barbadian singer [Rihanna](/wiki/Rihanna "Rihanna") is the most-followed musician and woman on X.
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Lifetimes_Tour_Manchester_52.jpg/250px-Lifetimes_Tour_Manchester_52.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lifetimes_Tour_Manchester_52.jpg)
American singer [Katy Perry](/wiki/Katy_Perry "Katy Perry") was first person to hit 100 million followers.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Recordings by American rapper and songwriter
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Kendrick_Lamar_%40_Grosse_Freiheit_36%2C_Hamburg_%289498448120%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Kendrick_Lamar_%40_Grosse_Freiheit_36%2C_Hamburg_%289498448120%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kendrick_Lamar_@_Grosse_Freiheit_36,_Hamburg_(9498448120)_(cropped).jpg)
[Kendrick Lamar](/wiki/Kendrick_Lamar "Kendrick Lamar") performing in [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg") in 2013
American rapper [Kendrick Lamar](/wiki/Kendrick_Lamar "Kendrick Lamar") has appeared in 74 [music videos](/wiki/Music_video "Music video"), 17 of which he directed with his creative partner [Dave Free](/wiki/Dave_Free "Dave Free").[[a]](#cite_note-2) He was featured in several [documentaries](/wiki/Documentary_film "Documentary film"), helmed three [short films](/wiki/Short_film "Short film"), and released one [concert film](/wiki/Concert_film "Concert film").
For his debut studio album, *[Section.80](/wiki/Section.80 "Section.80")* (2011), Lamar worked with directors such as [Vashtie Kola](/wiki/Vashtie_Kola "Vashtie Kola") to release four music videos, including visuals for "[HiiiPower](/wiki/HiiiPoWeR "HiiiPoWeR")" and "[A.D.H.D](/wiki/A.D.H.D_(Kendrick_Lamar_song) "A.D.H.D (Kendrick Lamar song)")".[[2]](#cite_note-3) His second album, *[Good Kid, M.A.A.D City](/wiki/Good_Kid,_M.A.A.D_City "Good Kid, M.A.A.D City")* (2012), was supported by music videos for "[Swimming Pools (Drank)](/wiki/Swimming_Pools_(Drank) "Swimming Pools (Drank)")", "[Backseat Freestyle](/wiki/Backseat_Freestyle "Backseat Freestyle")" (Lamar's [music video directorial](/wiki/Music_video_director "Music video director") debut), "[Poetic Justice](/wiki/Poetic_Justice_(song) "Poetic Justice (song)")", "[Bitch, Don't Kill My Vibe](/wiki/Bitch,_Don%27t_Kill_My_Vibe "Bitch, Don't Kill My Vibe")", and "[Sing About Me](/wiki/Sing_About_Me,_I%27m_Dying_of_Thirst "Sing About Me, I'm Dying of Thirst")". He also starred in and produced a companion short film, which was directed by [Khalil Joseph](/wiki/Kahlil_Joseph_(filmmaker) "Kahlil Joseph (filmmaker)").[[3]](#cite_note-4) Lamar and Free began receiving recognition for their directorial work with Lamar's third studio album, *[To Pimp a Butterfly](/wiki/To_Pimp_a_Butterfly "To Pimp a Butterfly")* (2015).[[4]](#cite_note-5)[[5]](#cite_note-6) Among its six visual components, "[Alright](/wiki/Alright_(Kendrick_Lamar_song) "Alright (Kendrick Lamar song)")" won [Best Direction](/wiki/MTV_Video_Music_Award_for_Best_Direction "MTV Video Music Award for Best Direction") at the [2015 MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/2015_MTV_Video_Music_Awards "2015 MTV Video Music Awards").[[6]](#cite_note-7) Lamar also won [Video of the Year](/wiki/MTV_Video_Music_Award_for_Video_of_the_Year "MTV Video Music Award for Video of the Year") and [Best Collaboration](/wiki/MTV_Video_Music_Award_for_Best_Collaboration "MTV Video Music Award for Best Collaboration") during the ceremony for his featured appearance on singer-songwriter [Taylor Swift](/wiki/Taylor_Swift "Taylor Swift")'s music video for "[Bad Blood](/wiki/Bad_Blood_(Taylor_Swift_song) "Bad Blood (Taylor Swift song)")".[[7]](#cite_note-8) Directed by [Joseph Kahn](/wiki/Joseph_Kahn_(director) "Joseph Kahn (director)"), it additionally received the [Grammy Award for Best Music Video](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Music_Video "Grammy Award for Best Music Video") in [2016](/wiki/58th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "58th Annual Grammy Awards").[[8]](#cite_note-9)
Lamar's fourth studio album, *[Damn](/wiki/Damn_(album) "Damn (album)")* (2017), was supported by music videos primarily directed by Lamar, Free and [Dave Meyers](/wiki/Dave_Meyers_(director) "Dave Meyers (director)").[[9]](#cite_note-10) The video for its lead single, "[Humble](/wiki/Humble_(song) "Humble (song)")", won six accolades at the [2017 MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/2017_MTV_Video_Music_Awards "2017 MTV Video Music Awards"), including Best Direction and Video of the Year. With his victory in the latter category, Lamar became the first artist to win Video of the Year for a video they co-directed.[[10]](#cite_note-11) "Humble" also received the Grammy Award for Best Music Video in [2018](/wiki/60th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "60th Annual Grammy Awards").[[11]](#cite_note-12) Lamar and Free directed the majority of the visuals for the former's fifth studio album, *[Mr. Morale & the Big Steppers](/wiki/Mr._Morale_%26_the_Big_Steppers "Mr. Morale & the Big Steppers")* (2022).[[12]](#cite_note-13)
Music videos
------------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kendrick_Lamar_videography&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Music videos")]
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Drake_July_2016.jpg/250px-Drake_July_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Drake_July_2016.jpg)
[Drake](/wiki/Drake_(musician) "Drake (musician)") appears on the songs and music videos for "[Poetic Justice](/wiki/Poetic_Justice_(song) "Poetic Justice (song)")" and "[Fuckin' Problems](/wiki/Fuckin%27_Problems "Fuckin' Problems")".
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Jay_Rock_-_Openair_Frauenfeld_2019_05.jpg/250px-Jay_Rock_-_Openair_Frauenfeld_2019_05.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jay_Rock_-_Openair_Frauenfeld_2019_05.jpg)
[Jay Rock](/wiki/Jay_Rock "Jay Rock") collaborated with Lamar on the songs and music videos for "[King's Dead](/wiki/King%27s_Dead "King's Dead")" "Money Trees", and "[Win](/wiki/Win_(song) "Win (song)")".
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Rihanna_Fenty_2018.png/250px-Rihanna_Fenty_2018.png)](/wiki/File:Rihanna_Fenty_2018.png)
[Rihanna](/wiki/Rihanna "Rihanna") features on the song and music video for "[Loyalty](/wiki/Loyalty_(Kendrick_Lamar_song) "Loyalty (Kendrick Lamar song)")".
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/SZA_CTRL_Tour_Toronto_2017_10_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-SZA_CTRL_Tour_Toronto_2017_10_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:SZA_CTRL_Tour_Toronto_2017_10_(cropped).jpg)
[SZA](/wiki/SZA "SZA") collaborated with Lamar on the song and music video for "[All the Stars](/wiki/All_the_Stars "All the Stars")" and "[Luther](/wiki/Luther_(song) "Luther (song)")".

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Chinese financial services company
.mw-parser-output .hatnote{font-style:italic}.mw-parser-output div.hatnote{padding-left:1.6em;margin-bottom:0.5em}.mw-parser-output .hatnote i{font-style:normal}.mw-parser-output .hatnote+link+.hatnote{margin-top:-0.5em}@media print{body.ns-0 .mw-parser-output .hatnote{display:none!important}}

This article is about the Chinese financial services company. For the mobile application developed by China UnionPay, see [UnionPay (application)](/wiki/UnionPay_(application) "UnionPay (application)").
.mw-parser-output .infobox-subbox{padding:0;border:none;margin:-3px;width:auto;min-width:100%;font-size:100%;clear:none;float:none;background-color:transparent;color:inherit}.mw-parser-output .infobox-3cols-child{margin:-3px}.mw-parser-output .infobox .navbar{font-size:100%}@media screen{html.skin-theme-clientpref-night .mw-parser-output .infobox-full-data:not(.notheme)>div:not(.notheme)[style]{background:#1f1f23!important;color:#f8f9fa}}@media screen and (prefers-color-scheme:dark){html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .infobox-full-data:not(.notheme)>div:not(.notheme)[style]{background:#1f1f23!important;color:#f8f9fa}}@media(min-width:640px){body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table{display:table!important}body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table>caption{display:table-caption!important}body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table>tbody{display:table-row-group}body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table th,body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table td{padding-left:inherit;padding-right:inherit}}.mw-parser-output .ib-company .infobox-label{padding-right:0.5em}.mw-parser-output .ib-company-logo img{background-color:#f8f9fa;color:inherit}.mw-parser-output .ib-company-logo .logo-nobg img{background-color:inherit;color:inherit}.mw-parser-output .ib-company-locality,.mw-parser-output .ib-company-country{display:inline}
UnionPay
|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| UnionPay Headquarters (Shanghai) | |
| Native name | 银联 (*Yínlián*) |
| Company type | [State-owned](/wiki/State-owned_enterprise "State-owned enterprise") |
| Industry | [Financial services](/wiki/Financial_services "Financial services") |
| Founded | 2002; 24 years ago (2002) |
| Headquarters | [Pudong](/wiki/Pudong "Pudong"), [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"), China |
| Area served | Worldwide |
| Key people | Shi Wenchao (president) |
| Products | [Payment systems](/wiki/Payment_system "Payment system") [Credit cards](/wiki/Credit_card "Credit card") |
| Number of employees | 559 (UnionPay International) |
| Website | [unionpayintl.com](https://www.unionpayintl.com/en/) |
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/UnionPay_coverage_map.svg/250px-UnionPay_coverage_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:UnionPay_coverage_map.svg)
Countries where the UnionPay payment system operates
**UnionPay** ([Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 银联; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Yínlián*), also known as **China UnionPay** ([Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 中国银联; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Zhōngguó Yínlián*) or by its abbreviation, **CUP** or **UPI** internationally, is a Chinese [state-owned](/wiki/State-owned_enterprise "State-owned enterprise") [financial services](/wiki/Financial_services "Financial services") corporation headquartered in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"), China. It provides [bank card](/wiki/Bank_card "Bank card") services and a major [card scheme](/wiki/Card_scheme "Card scheme") in [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China"). Founded on 26 March 2002, China UnionPay is an association for China's banking card industry, operating under the approval of the [People's Bank of China](/wiki/People%27s_Bank_of_China "People's Bank of China") (PBOC, central bank of China).[[1]](#cite_note-1) It is also an [electronic funds transfer](/wiki/Electronic_funds_transfer "Electronic funds transfer") at [point of sale](/wiki/Point_of_sale "Point of sale") ([EFTPOS](/wiki/EFTPOS "EFTPOS")) network, and the only [interbank network](/wiki/Interbank_network "Interbank network") in China that links all the [automatic teller machine](/wiki/Automatic_teller_machine "Automatic teller machine") (ATMs) of all [banks](/wiki/Bank "Bank") throughout the country. UnionPay claims that their cards can be used in "183 countries and regions around the world".[[2]](#cite_note-up-intro-2)
In 2015, the UnionPay overtook [Visa](/wiki/Visa_Inc. "Visa Inc.") and [Mastercard](/wiki/Mastercard "Mastercard") in total value of payments made by customers and became the largest card payment processing organization ([debit](/wiki/Debit_card "Debit card") and [credit cards](/wiki/Credit_card "Credit card") combined) in the world surpassing the two. In 2025, around 43% of UnionPay transactions occurred outside China[[3]](#cite_note-3) comparing to only 0.5% in 2015.[[4]](#cite_note-Finextra-4)
UnionPay offers [mobile](/wiki/Mobile_payment "Mobile payment") and [online payments](/wiki/Online_payment "Online payment") services.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)
History
-------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UnionPay&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/UnionPay_used_in_Chiba_20160212.jpg/250px-UnionPay_used_in_Chiba_20160212.jpg)](/wiki/File:UnionPay_used_in_Chiba_20160212.jpg)
UnionPay decal on the door of a dining hall in [Chiba](/wiki/Chiba_(city) "Chiba (city)"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan")
With the approval of the [People's Bank of China](/wiki/People%27s_Bank_of_China "People's Bank of China") (PBOC), China UnionPay was launched on 26 March 2002, in Shanghai by PBOC governor [Dai Xianglong](/wiki/Dai_Xianglong "Dai Xianglong"), The [Industrial and Commercial Bank of China](/wiki/Industrial_and_Commercial_Bank_of_China "Industrial and Commercial Bank of China"), the [Agricultural Bank of China](/wiki/Agricultural_Bank_of_China "Agricultural Bank of China"), the [Bank of China](/wiki/Bank_of_China "Bank of China") and the [China Construction Bank](/wiki/China_Construction_Bank "China Construction Bank") served as its first members.[[7]](#cite_note-UnionPay1-7) However, the concept of a unified Chinese bank card network dates back to 1993, with the formation of the "Golden Card Project" advocated by then-[Chinese leader](/wiki/Paramount_leader "Paramount leader") [Jiang Zemin](/wiki/Jiang_Zemin "Jiang Zemin"). UnionPay is considered its descendant, although attempts at unifying China's various credit card and interbank networks have been in place since the 1990s.[[8]](#cite_note-Links-8)
In 2014, UnionPay was reported to have been contributing to [capital flight](/wiki/Capital_flight "Capital flight") from China through poorly regulated store front operations in [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau").[[9]](#cite_note-9) The same year, the total amount of cross-bank transactions of CUP card exceeded 41.1 trillion yuan.[[10]](#cite_note-10) As part of [Xi Jinping's anti-corruption campaign](/wiki/Anti-corruption_campaign_under_Xi_Jinping "Anti-corruption campaign under Xi Jinping"), central government officials met with local officials in [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau") and reached an agreement in December 2014 that allows the central economic crimes bureau to have real-time access to all transactions in Macau involving UnionPay cards.[[11]](#cite_note-:1-11): 207
### Partnerships
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UnionPay&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Partnerships")]
Further information: [Card reciprocal agreements](/wiki/Card_reciprocal_agreements "Card reciprocal agreements")
In 2005, UnionPay entered into agreements with other payment networks to increase acceptance around the world. Some major examples include:
* [Discover](/wiki/Discover_Card "Discover Card") in United States[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* [RuPay](/wiki/RuPay "RuPay") in India [[13]](#cite_note-13)
* [JCB](/wiki/JCB_(credit_card_company) "JCB (credit card company)") in Japan[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [BC Card](/wiki/BC_Card "BC Card") in South Korea[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [Mir](/wiki/Mir_(payment_system) "Mir (payment system)") in Russia[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [Troy](/wiki/Troy_(card_scheme) "Troy (card scheme)") in Turkey [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
#### Use outside of China: Europe
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UnionPay&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Use outside of China: Europe")]
UnionPay announced in 2016 that more than 2.2 million merchants in 39 European countries and regions accept UnionPay, taking up 50% of all the merchants supporting bankcard payment.[[14]](#cite_note-Europe_acceptance-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Europe_hotel_acceptance-15)
Merchants from all sectors can integrate UnionPay and accept payments from their clients online or in-store. UnionPay is accepted at major department stores, airlines, duty-free stores and fashion stores, hotels, restaurants, historical and cultural sites, and entertainment establishments, such as bookstores and gift stores of le Louvre museum, Versailles, Orsay, Picasso, and Strasbourg Citrus Orchard.
UnionPay is also accepted at business schools and Universities in order to allow Asian students to pay large sums of money for their tuition fees.[[16]](#cite_note-Educational_instit_payment-16)
Merchants from certain sectors considered "High risk" or prohibited by the government cannot open a UnionPay account.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
##### Process for merchants accepting UnionPay in Europe
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UnionPay&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Process for merchants accepting UnionPay in Europe")]
Accepting UnionPay in Europe requires a third-party banking service.[[17]](#cite_note-How_to_accept_Unionpay-17) UnionPay tries to significantly multiply its partnerships to expand UnionPay's acceptance scope in Europe.
A European merchant can open a UnionPay account and start accepting payments through [payment gateways](/wiki/Payment_gateway "Payment gateway") which is licensed in the country of the merchant. Licensed payment gateways in [Eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone") countries are:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [Adyen](/wiki/Adyen "Adyen")
* [BNP Paribas](/wiki/BNP_Paribas "BNP Paribas")
* [Silkpay](/w/index.php?title=Silkpay&action=edit&redlink=1 "Silkpay (page does not exist)")
* [Stripe](/wiki/Stripe,_Inc. "Stripe, Inc.")[[18]](#cite_note-FTN1-18)
#### Use outside of China: rest of the world
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UnionPay&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Use outside of China: rest of the world")]
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/A_typical_easypaisa_unionpay_debit_card.jpg/250px-A_typical_easypaisa_unionpay_debit_card.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_typical_easypaisa_unionpay_debit_card.jpg)
The picture shows an EasyPaisa debit card powered by UnionPay. The card number and name are usually at the front.
Some UnionPay credit cards are also affiliated with [American Express](/wiki/American_Express "American Express") (AmEx), [MasterCard](/wiki/MasterCard "MasterCard"), or [Visa](/wiki/Visa_Inc. "Visa Inc."), and they can be used abroad as American Express, MasterCard, or Visa credit cards. UnionPay debit cards, however, can only be used in the UnionPay network and other networks that have signed contracts with UnionPay. Since 2006, China UnionPay cards can be used in over 100 countries outside China.[[19]](#cite_note-Foreign-19)
In May 2005, [Discover Network](/wiki/Discover_Network "Discover Network") announced an alliance with China UnionPay Network. The two companies have signed a long-term agreement that allows acceptance of Discover Network brand cards at UnionPay ATMs and point-of-sale terminals in China and acceptance of China UnionPay cards on the [PULSE](/wiki/Pulse_(interbank_network) "Pulse (interbank network)") network in the U.S.[[20]](#cite_note-Discover-20) As of 1 November 2007, China UnionPay cards may be accepted where Discover Network Cards are accepted in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), [Central America](/wiki/Central_America "Central America"), and the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean").[[21]](#cite_note-CardsInUS-21) As of early 2013, the cross acceptance agreement was expanded to support [e-commerce](/wiki/E-commerce "E-commerce") or [card-not-present transactions](/wiki/Card_not_present_transaction "Card not present transaction").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In March 2010, [PayPal](/wiki/PayPal "PayPal") announced a partnership with China UnionPay enabling the use of PayPal with UnionPay member cards.[[22]](#cite_note-22) In 2015, China's [State Administration of Foreign Exchange](/wiki/State_Administration_of_Foreign_Exchange "State Administration of Foreign Exchange") (SAFE) placed a ¥100,000 annual cap on overseas cash withdrawals from Chinese banks-issued UnionPay accounts.[[23]](#cite_note-23) In November 2017, [Azoya](/w/index.php?title=Azoya&action=edit&redlink=1 "Azoya (page does not exist)") teamed up with UnionPay to enable consumers in China to profit from the [Black Friday](/wiki/Black_Friday_(shopping) "Black Friday (shopping)") online shopping festival via a cross-border marketing platform.[[24]](#cite_note-24)
### 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UnionPay&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine")]
On 6 March 2022, [Sberbank](/wiki/Sberbank "Sberbank"), [Alfa Bank](/wiki/Alfa-Bank "Alfa-Bank") and [Tinkoff Bank](/wiki/Tinkoff_Bank "Tinkoff Bank") reported several Russian banks would switch to UnionPay after [Visa](/wiki/Visa_Inc. "Visa Inc.") and [Mastercard](/wiki/Mastercard "Mastercard") said they would suspend operations in Russia due to [Russia's invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine").[[25]](#cite_note-25) The Russian banks intend to tie the UnionPay system with Russia's [Mir](/wiki/Mir_(payment_system) "Mir (payment system)") network.[[26]](#cite_note-26)
On 20 April 2022, UnionPay reportedly discontinued cooperation with major Russian banks including Sberbank. Russian media outlet [RBC](/wiki/RBK_Daily "RBK Daily") stated that they did so out of fear of secondary sanctions.[[27]](#cite_note-27)
In August 2022, it was reported that Russian visitors to Finland were circumventing banking restrictions by withdrawing Euros from Finnish cash machines using UnionPay cards.[[28]](#cite_note-28)
Members
-------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UnionPay&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Members")]
See also: [List of banks](/wiki/List_of_banks "List of banks") and [List of largest banks](/wiki/List_of_largest_banks "List of largest banks")
UnionPay is the primary network of these Chinese banks:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [Agricultural Bank of China](/wiki/Agricultural_Bank_of_China "Agricultural Bank of China")
* [Bank of China](/wiki/Bank_of_China "Bank of China") (including its Hong Kong-based subsidiaries [Nanyang Commercial Bank](/wiki/Nanyang_Commercial_Bank "Nanyang Commercial Bank") and [BOC Hong Kong](/wiki/BOC_Hong_Kong "BOC Hong Kong"))
* [Bank of Communications](/wiki/Bank_of_Communications "Bank of Communications") (Credit cards co-issued with [HSBC](/wiki/HSBC_China "HSBC China")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*])
* [Bank of Beijing](/wiki/Bank_of_Beijing "Bank of Beijing")
* [Bank of Ningbo](/wiki/Bank_of_Ningbo "Bank of Ningbo")
* [Bank of Shanghai](/wiki/Bank_of_Shanghai "Bank of Shanghai")
* [China CITIC Bank](/wiki/China_CITIC_Bank "China CITIC Bank")
* [China Construction Bank](/wiki/China_Construction_Bank "China Construction Bank")
* [China Everbright Bank](/wiki/China_Everbright_Bank "China Everbright Bank")
* [China Merchants Bank](/wiki/China_Merchants_Bank "China Merchants Ba

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
.mw-parser-output .hatnote{font-style:italic}.mw-parser-output div.hatnote{padding-left:1.6em;margin-bottom:0.5em}.mw-parser-output .hatnote i{font-style:normal}.mw-parser-output .hatnote+link+.hatnote{margin-top:-0.5em}@media print{body.ns-0 .mw-parser-output .hatnote{display:none!important}}
For women's footballers, see [List of top international women's football goalscorers by country](/wiki/List_of_top_international_women%27s_football_goalscorers_by_country "List of top international women's football goalscorers by country").
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Euro_2016_Cristiano_Ronaldo.jpg/250px-Euro_2016_Cristiano_Ronaldo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Euro_2016_Cristiano_Ronaldo.jpg)
[Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo") of [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal_national_football_team "Portugal national football team") is the all-time leading goalscorer for men's national teams.
This article lists the top all-time goalscorer for each [men's national football team](/wiki/List_of_men%27s_national_association_football_teams "List of men's national association football teams"). This list is *not* an [all-time top international goalscorers list](/wiki/List_of_men%27s_footballers_with_50_or_more_international_goals "List of men's footballers with 50 or more international goals"), as several countries have two or more players with more goals than another country's top scorer. It simply lists only *the top scorer for each country*.
List of top scorers
-------------------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_top_international_men%27s_football_goalscorers_by_country&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: List of top scorers")]
:   Players in **bold** are still active at international level.
:   Players in *italics* also hold the record for most caps for their nation.
:   Rank is a count of the 211 FIFA nations. Fourteen nations (Bermuda, Brunei, Bulgaria, Denmark, Eritrea, Faroe Islands, Romania, Scotland, Sierra Leone, Somalia, South Sudan, Tanzania, United States and U.S. Virgin Islands) have a pair of players tied for national leader while Anguilla has three players tied as their leading scorer.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
A number of major airlines have declared [bankruptcy](/wiki/Bankruptcy "Bankruptcy") and have either ceased operations, or reorganized under [bankruptcy protection](/wiki/Reorganization "Reorganization"). Airlines, like any business, are susceptible to market fluctuations and economic difficulties. The economic structure of the airline industry may contribute to airline bankruptcies as well. One major element in almost every airline bankruptcy is the rejection by the debtor of its current collective bargaining agreements with employees. After satisfying certain requirements, bankruptcy law permits courts to approve the rejection of labor contracts by the debtor-employer. With this tool, airline managers reduce costs. Terms of an employee contract negotiated over years can be eliminated in months through [Chapter 11](/wiki/Chapter_11,_Title_11,_United_States_Code "Chapter 11, Title 11, United States Code"). Terms of the [Railway Labor Act](/wiki/Railway_Labor_Act "Railway Labor Act"), amended in 1936 to cover airlines, prevent most labor union work actions before, during and after an airline bankruptcy.
Continental Airlines declared bankruptcy, Chapter 11, a second time in December 1990.

he **38th British Academy Film Awards**, more commonly known as the [BAFTAs](/wiki/BAFTAs "BAFTAs"), took place on 5 March 1985 at the [Grosvenor House Hotel](/wiki/Grosvenor_House_Hotel "Grosvenor House Hotel") in [London](/wiki/London "London"), honouring the best national and foreign films of [1984](/wiki/1984_in_film "1984 in film"). Presented by the [British Academy of Film and Television Arts](/wiki/British_Academy_of_Film_and_Television_Arts "British Academy of Film and Television Arts"), accolades were handed out for the best feature-length film and documentaries of any nationality that were screened at British cinemas in 1984.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)
The evening's big winner was the British-made *[The Killing Fields](/wiki/The_Killing_Fields_(film) "The Killing Fields (film)")*, which had 13 nominations and won 8 of them.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The **PDC World Darts Championship**, officially known as the **Paddy Power World Darts Championship** for sponsorship reasons, is a world championship competition held annually in the sport of [darts](/wiki/Darts "Darts"). The championship begins in December and ends in January and has been held at [Alexandra Palace](/wiki/Alexandra_Palace "Alexandra Palace") in [London](/wiki/London "London") since 2008. Organised by the [Professional Darts Corporation](/wiki/Professional_Darts_Corporation "Professional Darts Corporation") (PDC), it is the most prestigious of their tournaments, with the winner receiving the Sid Waddell Trophy, named in honour of the darts commentator [Sid Waddell](/wiki/Sid_Waddell "Sid Waddell"). Along with the [Premier League Darts](/wiki/Premier_League_Darts "Premier League Darts") and [World Matchplay](/wiki/World_Matchplay_(darts) "World Matchplay (darts)"), it is part of the [Triple Crown](/wiki/Professional_Darts_Corporation_Triple_Crown "Professional Darts Corporation Triple Crown").
The PDC championship began in 1994 as the **WDC World Darts Championship** as one of the consequences of the [split in darts](/wiki/Split_in_darts "Split in darts"), which saw the [World Darts Council](/wiki/World_Darts_Council "World Darts Council") break away from the BDO. As a result of the settlement between the BDO and the WDC in 1997, the WDC became the PDC, and players were then free to choose which world championship to enter (but not both in the same year), as long as they met certain eligibility criteria. Both organisations continued to organize their own world championship until the 2020 editions, after which the BDO folded.
There have been thirteen different winners since the tournament's inception. With 14 wins from 25 appearances, [Phil Taylor](/wiki/Phil_Taylor_(darts_player) "Phil Taylor (darts player)") dominated the competition until last winning it in 2013. Other players to win more than once are [John Part](/wiki/John_Part "John Part") (2003 & 2008), [Adrian Lewis](/wiki/Adrian_Lewis "Adrian Lewis") (2011 & 2012), [Gary Anderson](/wiki/Gary_Anderson_(darts_player) "Gary Anderson (darts player)") (2015 & 2016), [Michael van Gerwen](/wiki/Michael_van_Gerwen "Michael van Gerwen") (2014, 2017 & 2019), [Peter Wright](/wiki/Peter_Wright_(darts_player) "Peter Wright (darts player)") (2020 & 2022) and [Luke Littler](/wiki/Luke_Littler "Luke Littler") (2025 & 2026). The one-time winners are the inaugural champion [Dennis Priestley](/wiki/Dennis_Priestley "Dennis Priestley") (1994), [Raymond van Barneveld](/wiki/Raymond_van_Barneveld "Raymond van Barneveld") (2007), [Rob Cross](/wiki/Rob_Cross_(darts_player) "Rob Cross (darts player)") (2018), [Gerwyn Price](/wiki/Gerwyn_Price "Gerwyn Price") (2021),  [Michael Smith](/wiki/Michael_Smith_(darts_player) "Michael Smith (darts player)") (2023) and [Luke Humphries](/wiki/Luke_Humphries "Luke Humphries") (2024).
History
-------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=PDC_World_Darts_Championship&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/2009_World_Darts_Championship.jpg/250px-2009_World_Darts_Championship.jpg)](/wiki/File:2009_World_Darts_Championship.jpg)
A match between [Mensur Suljović](/wiki/Mensur_Suljovi%C4%87 "Mensur Suljović") and [Kevin Painter](/wiki/Kevin_Painter "Kevin Painter") at the [2010 championship](/wiki/2010_PDC_World_Darts_Championship "2010 PDC World Darts Championship")
In 1992, [some high-profile players](/wiki/1994_PDC_World_Darts_Championship#The_16_Defectors "1994 PDC World Darts Championship"), including all previous winners of the [BDO World Darts Championship](/wiki/BDO_World_Darts_Championship "BDO World Darts Championship") still active in the game, formed the WDC, and in 1994, held their first World Championship. [Dennis Priestley](/wiki/Dennis_Priestley "Dennis Priestley") won the inaugural competition.
The players who broke away were taking a significant gamble – the tournament was broadcast on satellite television rather than terrestrial, and from 1994 to 2001, the prize fund for the players in the WDC/PDC World Championship was lower than the prize fund in the BDO version, although the 1997 PDC World Champion received £45,000 compared to that year's BDO World Champion receiving £38,000. In 2002, the PDC prize fund overtook that of the BDO for the first time, and the PDC event now boasts the largest prize fund of any darts competition. In 2010, the prize fund reached £1 million for the first time, with the champion collecting £200,000.
The 2014 and 2015 PDC world champions collected £250,000 for their wins. For the next three years, the winner's share increased by £50,000 each year culminating in a 2018 prize fund of £1.8 million.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The prize fund for the tournament was £2.5 million with £500,000 to the winner from 2019. The 2025/26 World Championship will see the field expand to 128 players with the prize fund increasing to £5 million with a record £1 million to the champion.
In the [2001 PDC World Darts Championship](/wiki/2001_PDC_World_Darts_Championship "2001 PDC World Darts Championship") [Gayl King](/wiki/Gayl_King "Gayl King") became the first woman to play in the PDC World Darts Championship. Since then [Anastasia Dobromyslova](/wiki/Anastasia_Dobromyslova "Anastasia Dobromyslova"), [Deta Hedman](/wiki/Deta_Hedman "Deta Hedman"), [Lisa Ashton](/wiki/Lisa_Ashton "Lisa Ashton"), [Fallon Sherrock](/wiki/Fallon_Sherrock "Fallon Sherrock"), [Mikuru Suzuki](/wiki/Mikuru_Suzuki "Mikuru Suzuki"), [Beau Greaves](/wiki/Beau_Greaves "Beau Greaves") and [Noa-Lynn van Leuven](/wiki/Noa-Lynn_van_Leuven "Noa-Lynn van Leuven") have all played in the PDC World Darts Championship. In the [2020 PDC World Darts Championship](/wiki/2020_PDC_World_Darts_Championship "2020 PDC World Darts Championship") Sherrock became the first woman to defeat a man in the PDC World Darts Championship, when she defeated [Ted Evetts](/wiki/Ted_Evetts "Ted Evetts"). In the next round she also defeated [Mensur Suljovic](/wiki/Mensur_Suljovic "Mensur Suljovic") before losing to [Chris Dobey](/wiki/Chris_Dobey "Chris Dobey"). Sherrock remains the only woman to defeat a man in the PDC World Darts Championship.
Venue
-----
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=PDC_World_Darts_Championship&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Venue")]
The tournament has been held at [Alexandra Palace](/wiki/Alexandra_Palace "Alexandra Palace") in [London](/wiki/London "London") since 2008, having previously been held at the [Circus Tavern](/wiki/Circus_Tavern "Circus Tavern") in [Purfleet](/wiki/Purfleet "Purfleet"), [Essex](/wiki/Essex "Essex"), from 1994 to 2007.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Currently, the play takes place inside the venue's West Hall, which has a capacity of 3,200.[[3]](#cite_note-3) From the edition of 2027, the play will take place at the Great Hall, which expands the capacity to 5,000.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

question: How many state-level felonies has the most recent former president of the United States been convicted of?
answer: Joe Biden, the most recent former president of the United States, hasn't been convicted of any state-level felonies.

The west facade of the [Naikaku Sōri Daijin Kantei](/wiki/Naikaku_S%C5%8Dri_Daijin_Kantei "Naikaku Sōri Daijin Kantei") (valley side), the office of the prime minister
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/IT%C5%8C_Hirobumi.jpg/250px-IT%C5%8C_Hirobumi.jpg)](/wiki/File:IT%C5%8C_Hirobumi.jpg)
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Hideki_Tojo_2_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Hideki_Tojo_2_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hideki_Tojo_2_(cropped).jpg)
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Shinz%C5%8D_Abe_20200101.jpg/250px-Shinz%C5%8D_Abe_20200101.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shinz%C5%8D_Abe_20200101.jpg)
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Official_portrait_of_Sanae_Takaichi%2C_Prime_Minister_of_Japan_%28HD%29.jpg/250px-Official_portrait_of_Sanae_Takaichi%2C_Prime_Minister_of_Japan_%28HD%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Official_portrait_of_Sanae_Takaichi,_Prime_Minister_of_Japan_(HD).jpg)
* [Itō Hirobumi](/wiki/It%C5%8D_Hirobumi "Itō Hirobumi") (top left) is considered the first prime minister of Japan.
* [Hideki Tojo](/wiki/Hideki_Tojo "Hideki Tojo") (top right) was prime minister during much of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").
* [Shinzo Abe](/wiki/Shinzo_Abe "Shinzo Abe") (bottom left) was the longest-serving prime minister in Japanese history.
* [Sanae Takaichi](/wiki/Sanae_Takaichi "Sanae Takaichi") (bottom right) is the first female and incumbent prime minister of Japan.
The [prime minister of Japan](/wiki/Prime_minister_of_Japan "Prime minister of Japan") is the country's [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") and the leader of the [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Japan "Cabinet of Japan"). This is a list of prime ministers of [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), from when the first Japanese prime minister (in the modern sense), [Itō Hirobumi](/wiki/It%C5%8D_Hirobumi "Itō Hirobumi"), took office in 1885, until the present day. 32 prime ministers under the [Meiji Constitution](/wiki/Meiji_Constitution "Meiji Constitution") had a [mandate](/wiki/Mandate_(politics) "Mandate (politics)") from the [Emperor](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan"). The "electoral mandates" shown are for the [House of Representatives](/wiki/House_of_Representatives_(Japan) "House of Representatives (Japan)"), the lower house of the Imperial Diet, which was not constitutionally guaranteed to have any influence on the appointment of the prime minister.
The prime minister under the [Constitution of Japan](/wiki/Constitution_of_Japan "Constitution of Japan") is designated from among the members of the [National Diet](/wiki/National_Diet "National Diet"), and appointed by the Emperor after being nominated by the National Diet. The premiership of [Prince Naruhiko Higashikuni](/wiki/Prince_Naruhiko_Higashikuni "Prince Naruhiko Higashikuni"), who was prime minister for fifty-four days in 1945, was the shortest in Japanese history. [Shinzo Abe](/wiki/Shinzo_Abe "Shinzo Abe") served the longest, with eight years over two non-consecutive periods. The incumbent and first female prime minister is [Sanae Takaichi](/wiki/Sanae_Takaichi "Sanae Takaichi"), who assumed the office on 21 October 2025.
From 1871 to 1885
-----------------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_prime_ministers_of_Japan&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: From 1871 to 1885")]
The office of [daijō-daijin](/wiki/Daij%C5%8D-daijin "Daijō-daijin") (太政大臣, chancellor of the realm) was the equivalent of what would become the office of prime minister. It was an ancient role which had been discontinued in the 18th century and was briefly revived during the [Meiji era](/wiki/Meiji_era "Meiji era").
### Daijō-daijin (chancellor of the realm)
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_prime_ministers_of_Japan&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Daijō-daijin (chancellor of the realm)")]
List of daijō-daijin (chancellor of the realm) (1871–1885)
| Portrait | | Daijō-daijin Office (Lifespan) | Term of office | | | Mandate | Party | [Government](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Japan "Cabinet of Japan") | [Emperor](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan") Reign |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Start | End | Duration |
|  | [photograph](/wiki/File:Sanetomi_Sanjo_(3x4_cropped).jpg "photograph") | [Prince **Sanjō Sanetomi**](/wiki/Sanj%C5%8D_Sanetomi "Sanjō Sanetomi") 三條 實美 (1837–1891) | 13 September 1871 | 22 December 1885 | 14 years, 101 days | — | *[Independent](/wiki/Independent_politician "Independent politician")* |  | [Meiji](/wiki/Emperor_Meiji "Emperor Meiji")[Emperor Meiji](/wiki/File:Meiji_tenno1.jpg)     r. 1867–1912 ([Meiji era](/wiki/Meiji_era "Meiji era")) |
The office of Daijō-daijin was abolished in December 1885 with the appointment of [Itō Hirobumi](/wiki/It%C5%8D_Hirobumi "Itō Hirobumi") in the new position of prime minister of Japan (内閣総理大臣, Naikaku Sōri-Daijin).
