From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Los_Angeles_Metro_System_Map.png/500px-Los_Angeles_Metro_System_Map.png)](/wiki/File:Los_Angeles_Metro_System_Map.png)
Metro Rail and Busway system map
[Metro Rail](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Metro_Rail "Los Angeles Metro Rail") is an [urban rail transit](/wiki/Urban_rail_transit "Urban rail transit") system in [Los Angeles County, California](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County,_California "Los Angeles County, California"). It is operated by [Los Angeles Metro](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Metro "Los Angeles Metro"). The system includes 107 [metro stations](/wiki/Metro_station "Metro station") with two [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") and four [light rail](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail") lines, covering 121.2 miles (195.1 km) of route service.[[1]](#cite_note-FactsGlance-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) In 2019, the Metro Rail system served an average 295,889 passengers each weekday, totaling 93.2 million passengers in the calendar year.[[3]](#cite_note-ridership-3) Metro Rail is one of the largest [rapid transit](/wiki/List_of_United_States_rapid_transit_systems_by_ridership "List of United States rapid transit systems by ridership") and [light rail](/wiki/List_of_United_States_light_rail_systems_by_ridership "List of United States light rail systems by ridership") systems in the United States by ridership.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The system is complemented by two [Metro Busway](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Metro_Busway "Los Angeles Metro Busway") [bus rapid transit](/wiki/Bus_rapid_transit "Bus rapid transit") lines.
History
-------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_Los_Angeles_Metro_Rail_stations&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]
Metro Rail began service on July 14, 1990, when the light rail [Blue Line](/wiki/A_Line_(Los_Angeles_Metro) "A Line (Los Angeles Metro)") opened between [Pico](/wiki/Pico_station "Pico station") and [Anaheim Street](/wiki/Anaheim_Street_station "Anaheim Street station") stations;[[5]](#cite_note-blue-5) the line was extended to [Downtown Long Beach](/wiki/Downtown_Long_Beach_station "Downtown Long Beach station") and [Pacific Avenue](/wiki/Pacific_Avenue_station "Pacific Avenue station") stations on September 1.[[6]](#cite_note-longbeach-6) The Blue Line was extended one stop northward from Pico to [7th Street/​Metro Center](/wiki/7th_Street/Metro_Center_station "7th Street/Metro Center station") on February 15, 1991.[[7]](#cite_note-blue2-7) The next Metro Rail line, the rapid transit [Red Line](/wiki/B_Line_(Los_Angeles_Metro) "B Line (Los Angeles Metro)"), opened on January 30, 1993, between [Union Station](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Union_Station "Los Angeles Union Station") and [Westlake/​MacArthur Park](/wiki/Westlake/MacArthur_Park_station "Westlake/MacArthur Park station") station.[[8]](#cite_note-red-8) The light rail [Green Line](/wiki/C_Line_(Los_Angeles_Metro) "C Line (Los Angeles Metro)"), the system's third line, opened on August 12, 1995, from [Norwalk](/wiki/Norwalk_station_(Los_Angeles_Metro) "Norwalk station (Los Angeles Metro)") to [Redondo Beach](/wiki/Redondo_Beach_station "Redondo Beach station") stations.[[9]](#cite_note-green-9) Metro Rail's next expansion occurred on May 22, 1996, when the Red Line expanded westward from Westlake/MacArthur Park to [Wilshire/​Western](/wiki/Wilshire/Western_station "Wilshire/Western station") stations.[[10]](#cite_note-red2-10) The Red Line expanded again on June 12, 1999, with a branch from [Wilshire/​Vermont](/wiki/Wilshire/Vermont_station "Wilshire/Vermont station") to [Hollywood/​Vine](/wiki/Hollywood/Vine_station "Hollywood/Vine station") stations.[[11]](#cite_note-red3-11) The final section of the Red Line opened on June 24, 2000, from Hollywood/Vine station to [North Hollywood](/wiki/North_Hollywood_station "North Hollywood station") station, completing the Red Line as originally planned.[[12]](#cite_note-red4-12) A fourth Metro Rail line, the light rail [Gold Line](/wiki/L_Line_(Los_Angeles_Metro) "L Line (Los Angeles Metro)"), opened on July 27, 2003, between Union Station and [Sierra Madre Villa](/wiki/Sierra_Madre_Villa_station "Sierra Madre Villa station") station in [Pasadena](/wiki/Pasadena "Pasadena").[[13]](#cite_note-gold-13) The rapid transit [Purple Line](/wiki/D_Line_(Los_Angeles_Metro) "D Line (Los Angeles Metro)") became the fifth Metro Rail line on August 24, 2006, when Los Angeles Metro separated the Red Line into two separate services; the branch between Union Station and Wilshire/Western station became the Purple Line while the branch between Union Station and North Hollywood station remained the Red Line.[[14]](#cite_note-purple-14) The Gold Line was later extended to [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_station_(Los_Angeles_Metro) "Atlantic station (Los Angeles Metro)") station in [East Los Angeles](/wiki/East_Los_Angeles "East Los Angeles") on November 15, 2009.[[15]](#cite_note-gold2-15) The light rail [Expo Line](/wiki/E_Line_(Los_Angeles_Metro) "E Line (Los Angeles Metro)") opened between 7th Street/Metro Center and [La Cienega/​Jefferson](/wiki/La_Cienega/Jefferson_station "La Cienega/Jefferson station") on April 28, 2012; two additional stations opened on June 20, 2012.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The Gold Line's second extension opened on March 5, 2016, and added six more stations from Sierra Madre Villa from to [APU/​Citrus College](/wiki/APU/Citrus_College_station "APU/Citrus College station").[[17]](#cite_note-Gold_Line_Foothill_Extension_opening-17) An extension to the Expo Line on May 20, 2016, added seven stations.[[18]](#cite_note-Expo_Line_Phase_2-18) The opening of the K Line on October 7, 2022, added six stations.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The [Regional Connector](/wiki/Regional_Connector "Regional Connector") project featured two new underground stations as well as a rebuilt Little Tokyo/Arts District station. Aviation/Century station opened on November 3, 2024.[[20]](#cite_note-CKreconfig-20) The LAX/Metro Transit Center opened on June 6, 2025.[[21]](#cite_note-laxopen-21) The first phase of the A Line Foothill Extension, consisting of four new stations and 9.1 mi (14.6 km) of track, opened on September 19, 2025.[[22]](#cite_note-pomonaopen-22)
System
------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_Los_Angeles_Metro_Rail_stations&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: System")]
The system has 107 stations serving its six lines. Fourteen of these stations are transfer stations, which allow passengers to transfer between lines. Eleven of these stations are [termini](/wiki/Train_station#Terminus "Train station")—stations at the end of lines. 55 of the stations are within the city of Los Angeles and the other 52 stations are located in surrounding communities in Los Angeles County.
### Lines
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_Los_Angeles_Metro_Rail_stations&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Lines")]
There are six Metro Rail lines, each of which is associated with a letter.

The **Ballon d'Or** (.mw-parser-output .IPA-label-small{font-size:85%}.mw-parser-output .references .IPA-label-small,.mw-parser-output .infobox .IPA-label-small,.mw-parser-output .navbox .IPA-label-small{font-size:100%}French pronunciation: [[balɔ̃ dɔʁ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q150_(fra)-Touam-Ballon_d%27Or.wav "File:LL-Q150 (fra)-Touam-Ballon d'Or.wav"); lit. '**Golden Ball**') is an annual [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") award presented by French magazine *[France Football](/wiki/France_Football "France Football")* since [1956](/wiki/1956_Ballon_d%27Or "1956 Ballon d'Or") to honour the [player](/wiki/Football_player "Football player") judged to have performed the best over the previous [season](/wiki/Season_(sports) "Season (sports)").
Conceived by sports writers [Gabriel Hanot](/wiki/Gabriel_Hanot "Gabriel Hanot") and [Jacques Ferran](/wiki/Jacques_Ferran "Jacques Ferran"), the Ballon d'Or was based exclusively on voting by football journalists up until [2006](/wiki/2006_Ballon_d%27Or "2006 Ballon d'Or"). Originally, it was awarded only to players from Europe and was also known as the **European Footballer of the Year** award. In [1995](/wiki/1995_Ballon_d%27Or "1995 Ballon d'Or"), the Ballon d'Or was expanded to include all players of any origin active at European clubs. The award became a global prize in [2007](/wiki/2007_Ballon_d%27Or "2007 Ballon d'Or") with all professional footballers being eligible; additionally, coaches and captains of national teams were also given the right to vote, before reverting to just journalists in [2016](/wiki/2016_Ballon_d%27Or "2016 Ballon d'Or").
Between [2010](/wiki/2010_FIFA_Ballon_d%27Or "2010 FIFA Ballon d'Or") and [2015](/wiki/2015_FIFA_Ballon_d%27Or "2015 FIFA Ballon d'Or"), in an agreement with [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA"), the award was merged with the [FIFA World Player of the Year](/wiki/FIFA_World_Player_of_the_Year "FIFA World Player of the Year") and rebranded as the [FIFA Ballon d'Or](/wiki/FIFA_Ballon_d%27Or "FIFA Ballon d'Or"). That partnership ended in 2016, and the award reverted to the Ballon d'Or, while FIFA also reinstated its own separate annual award, [The Best FIFA Men's Player](/wiki/The_Best_FIFA_Men%27s_Player "The Best FIFA Men's Player"). In [2022](/wiki/2022_Ballon_d%27Or "2022 Ballon d'Or"), *France Football* modified the rules for the Ballon d'Or. The timing was changed so that the award is based on a football season rather than a calendar year, and an international jury of specialized journalists—one representative per country from the top 100 in the latest [FIFA Men's World Ranking](/wiki/FIFA_Men%27s_World_Ranking "FIFA Men's World Ranking")—was introduced to determine the winner. [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") has co-organized the Ballon d'Or gala since [2024](/wiki/2024_Ballon_d%27Or "2024 Ballon d'Or"), with *France Football* retaining the voting system and the Ballon d'Or name.
[Lionel Messi](/wiki/Lionel_Messi "Lionel Messi") has won the Ballon d'Or a record eight times, followed by [Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo") with five. [Johan Cruyff](/wiki/Johan_Cruyff "Johan Cruyff"), [Michel Platini](/wiki/Michel_Platini "Michel Platini") and [Marco van Basten](/wiki/Marco_van_Basten "Marco van Basten") have each won the award three times, while [Alfredo Di Stéfano](/wiki/Alfredo_Di_St%C3%A9fano "Alfredo Di Stéfano"), [Franz Beckenbauer](/wiki/Franz_Beckenbauer "Franz Beckenbauer"), [Kevin Keegan](/wiki/Kevin_Keegan "Kevin Keegan"), [Karl-Heinz Rummenigge](/wiki/Karl-Heinz_Rummenigge "Karl-Heinz Rummenigge") and [Ronaldo](/wiki/Ronaldo_(Brazilian_footballer) "Ronaldo (Brazilian footballer)") have each won it twice. [Ousmane Dembélé](/wiki/Ousmane_Demb%C3%A9l%C3%A9 "Ousmane Dembélé") is the current holder of the award, having won [its 69th edition](/wiki/2025_Ballon_d%27Or "2025 Ballon d'Or") in 2025.

Overview
--------
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/d/d1/1956_France_Football_magazine_cover.jpg/250px-1956_France_Football_magazine_cover.jpg)](/wiki/File:1956_France_Football_magazine_cover.jpg)
December 1956 issue of *[France Football](/wiki/France_Football "France Football")* magazine presenting the [inaugural Ballon d'Or](/wiki/1956_Ballon_d%27Or "1956 Ballon d'Or") winner—[Stanley Matthews](/wiki/Stanley_Matthews "Stanley Matthews").
The Ballon d'Or is widely regarded as the most prestigious individual award in football.[[1]](#cite_note-DiarioAS2023-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Goal2023-2)[[3]](#cite_note-TheSportingNews2025-3) Prior to [2007](/wiki/2007_Ballon_d%27Or "2007 Ballon d'Or"), it was based exclusively on voting by football journalists and was also known as the continental European Footballer of the Year award.[[4]](#cite_note-WorldSoccer2007-4)[[5]](#cite_note-PerspectivesonPolitics2020-5) Even after 2007, it was usually identified with and referred to by that name because of its origin as a European award,[[6]](#cite_note-BBCSport2008a-6)[[7]](#cite_note-TheTelegraph2008-7) until it was merged with the [FIFA World Player of the Year](/wiki/FIFA_World_Player_of_the_Year "FIFA World Player of the Year") award cementing its new worldwide claim.[[8]](#cite_note-TheGuardian2007-8)[[9]](#cite_note-FIFA2009-9) [Stanley Matthews](/wiki/Stanley_Matthews "Stanley Matthews") of England was the [inaugural winner](/wiki/1956_Ballon_d%27Or "1956 Ballon d'Or") of the award, and the oldest player to ever win it—at 41 years, 321 days old.[[10]](#cite_note-UEFA2025-10)[[11]](#cite_note-BBCSport2008b-11) Liberia's [George Weah](/wiki/George_Weah "George Weah"), the only African recipient, became the first non-European to win the award in [1995](/wiki/1995_Ballon_d%27Or "1995 Ballon d'Or"), the year that rules of eligibility were changed and the Ballon d'Or was expanded to include all players of any origin, active at European clubs;[[12]](#cite_note-BBCSport2008c-12)[[13]](#cite_note-NBCSports2023-13) [two years later](/wiki/1997_Ballon_d%27Or "1997 Ballon d'Or"), [Ronaldo](/wiki/Ronaldo_(Brazilian_footballer) "Ronaldo (Brazilian footballer)") of Brazil became the first South American without a European citizenship to claim the award,[[12]](#cite_note-BBCSport2008c-12) and he is still the youngest winner ever at 21 years, 96 days old.[[14]](#cite_note-ESPN2023-14) The award became a global prize in 2007 with all professional footballers from clubs around the world being eligible;[[15]](#cite_note-BBCSport2008d-15) additionally, coaches and captains of national teams were also given the right to vote,[[5]](#cite_note-PerspectivesonPolitics2020-5)[[14]](#cite_note-ESPN2023-14) before reverting to just journalists in [2016](/wiki/2016_Ballon_d%27Or "2016 Ballon d'Or").[[16]](#cite_note-TheAth

This is a list of works (films, television, shorts etc.) by the Japanese [animation studio](/wiki/Animation_studio "Animation studio") [Studio Ghibli](/wiki/Studio_Ghibli "Studio Ghibli").
Works
-----
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_Studio_Ghibli_works&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Works")]
### Feature films

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1986 | *[Castle in the Sky](/wiki/Castle_in_the_Sky "Castle in the Sky")* | 天空の城ラピュタ | [Hayao Miyazaki](/wiki/Hayao_Miyazaki "Hayao Miyazaki") | | [Isao Takahata](/wiki/Isao_Takahata "Isao Takahata") | [Joe Hisaishi](/wiki/Joe_Hisaishi "Joe Hisaishi") | Japan: August 2, 1986 | 125 minutes |

| 1991 | *[Only Yesterday](/wiki/Only_Yesterday_(1991_film) "Only Yesterday (1991 film)")* | おもひでぽろぽろ | Isao Takahata | | [Toshio Suzuki](/wiki/Toshio_Suzuki_(producer) "Toshio Suzuki (producer)") | Masaru Hoshi | Japan: July 20, 1991 English dub premiere: January 1, 2016 | 119 minutes |
| 1992 | *[Porco Rosso](/wiki/Porco_Rosso "Porco Rosso")* | 紅の豚 | Hayao Miyazaki | | Joe Hisaishi | Japan: July 18, 1992 | 93 minutes |
| 1994 | *[Pom Poko](/wiki/Pom_Poko "Pom Poko")* | 平成狸合戦ぽんぽこ | Isao Takahata | | [Shang Shang Typhoon](/wiki/Shang_Shang_Typhoon "Shang Shang Typhoon") | Japan: July 16, 1994 | 119 minutes |
| 1995 | *[Whisper of the Heart](/wiki/Whisper_of_the_Heart "Whisper of the Heart")* | 耳をすませば | [Yoshifumi Kondō](/wiki/Yoshifumi_Kond%C5%8D "Yoshifumi Kondō") | Hayao Miyazaki | [Yuji Nomi](/wiki/Yuji_Nomi "Yuji Nomi") | Japan: July 15, 1995 | 111 minutes |
| 1997 | *[Princess Mononoke](/wiki/Princess_Mononoke "Princess Mononoke")* | もののけ姫 | Hayao Miyazaki | | Joe Hisaishi | Japan: July 12, 1997 United States: October 29, 1999 | 133 minutes |
| 1999 | *[My Neighbors the Yamadas](/wiki/My_Neighbors_the_Yamadas "My Neighbors the Yamadas")* | ホーホケキョ となりの山田くん | Isao Takahata | | [Akiko Yano](/wiki/Akiko_Yano "Akiko Yano") | Japan: July 17, 1999 | 103 minutes |
| 2001 | *[Spirited Away](/wiki/Spirited_Away "Spirited Away")* | 千と千尋の神隠し | Hayao Miyazaki | | Joe Hisaishi | Japan: July 20, 2001 United States: September 20, 2002 | 125 minutes |
| 2002 | *[The Cat Returns](/wiki/The_Cat_Returns "The Cat Returns")* | 猫の恩返し | [Hiroyuki Morita](/wiki/Hiroyuki_Morita "Hiroyuki Morita") | [Reiko Yoshida](/wiki/Reiko_Yoshida "Reiko Yoshida") | Nozomu Takahashi and Toshio Suzuki | Yuji Nomi | Japan: July 19, 2002 | 75 minutes |
| 2004 | *[Howl's Moving Castle](/wiki/Howl%27s_Moving_Castle_(film) "Howl's Moving Castle (film)")* | ハウルの動く城 | Hayao Miyazaki | | Toshio Suzuki | Joe Hisaishi | Japan: November 20, 2004 United States: June 10, 2005 | 119 minutes |
| 2006 | *[Tales from Earthsea](/wiki/Tales_from_Earthsea_(film) "Tales from Earthsea (film)")* | ゲド戦記 | [Gorō Miyazaki](/wiki/Gor%C5%8D_Miyazaki "Gorō Miyazaki") | Gorō Miyazaki and Keiko Niwa | Tamiya Terashima | Japan: July 29, 2006 United States: August 13, 2010 | 115 minutes |
| 2008 | *[Ponyo](/wiki/Ponyo "Ponyo")* | 崖の上のポニョ | Hayao Miyazaki | | Joe Hisaishi | Japan: July 19, 2008 United States: August 14, 2009 | 101 minutes |
| 2010 | *[Arrietty](/wiki/Arrietty "Arrietty")* | 借りぐらしのアリエッティ | [Hiromasa Yonebayashi](/wiki/Hiromasa_Yonebayashi "Hiromasa Yonebayashi") | Hayao Miyazaki and Keiko Niwa | [Cécile Corbel](/wiki/C%C3%A9cile_Corbel "Cécile Corbel") | Japan: July 17, 2010 United States: February 17, 2012 | 94 minutes |

| 2013 | *[The Wind Rises](/wiki/The_Wind_Rises "The Wind Rises")*[[1]](#cite_note-AsahiShimbun-1) | 風立ちぬ | Hayao Miyazaki | | Joe Hisaishi | Japan: July 20, 2013 United States: February 21, 2014 | 126 minutes |
| *[The Tale of the Princess Kaguya](/wiki/The_Tale_of_the_Princess_Kaguya_(film) "The Tale of the Princess Kaguya (film)")*[[1]](#cite_note-AsahiShimbun-1) | かぐや姫の物語 | Isao Takahata | Isao Takahata and Riko Sakaguchi | [Yoshiaki Nishimura](/wiki/Yoshiaki_Nishimura "Yoshiaki Nishimura") | Japan: November 23, 2013 United States: October 17, 2014 | 137 minutes |
| 2014 | *[When Marnie Was There](/wiki/When_Marnie_Was_There_(film) "When Marnie Was There (film)")*[[2]](#cite_note-2) | 思い出のマーニー | Hiromasa Yonebayashi | Keiko Niwa, [Masashi Ando](/wiki/Masashi_Ando "Masashi Ando") and Hiromasa Yonebayashi | [Takatsugu Muramatsu](/wiki/Takatsugu_Muramatsu "Takatsugu Muramatsu") | Japan: July 19, 2014 United States: May 22, 2015 | 103 minutes |
| 2016 | *[The Red Turtle](/wiki/The_Red_Turtle "The Red Turtle")*[[a]](#cite_note-4) | レッドタートル ある島の物語 | [Michaël Dudok de Wit](/wiki/Micha%C3%ABl_Dudok_de_Wit "Michaël Dudok de Wit") | [Michaël Dudok de Wit](/wiki/Micha%C3%ABl_Dudok_de_Wit "Michaël Dudok de Wit") and [Pascale Ferran](/wiki/Pascale_Ferran "Pascale Ferran") | Toshio Suzuki, Isao Takahata, Vincent Maraval, Pascal Caucheteux and Grégoire Sorlat | Laurent Perez del Mar | May 18, 2016 | 80 minutes |
| 2023 | *[The Boy and the Heron](/wiki/The_Boy_and_the_Heron "The Boy and the Heron")* | 君たちはどう生きるか | Hayao Miyazaki | | Toshio Suzuki | Joe Hisaishi | Japan: July 14, 2023 United States: December 8, 2023 | 124 minutes |

| 2002 | *[Ghiblies Episode 2](/wiki/Ghiblies_Episode_2 "Ghiblies Episode 2")* | Yoshiyuki Momose |  | Shown theatrically before *[The Cat Returns](/wiki/The_Cat_Returns "The Cat Returns")* |
| *[Koro's Big Day Out](/wiki/Koro%27s_Big_Day_Out "Koro's Big Day Out")* | Hayao Miyazaki |  | Ghibli Museum |
| *[Imaginary Flying Machines](/wiki/Imaginary_Flying_Machines "Imaginary Flying Machines")* | Toshio Suzuki |
| *[Mei And The Kittenbus](/wiki/Short_films_by_Studio_Ghibli#Mei_and_the_Kittenbus "Short films by Studio Ghibli")* |  |

| *[Space Station No. 9](/w/index.php?title=Space_Station_No._9&action=edit&redlink=1 "Space Station No. 9 (page does not exist)")* |  |

| *[Soratobu Toshikeikaku](/w/index.php?title=Soratobu_Toshikeikaku&action=edit&redlink=1 "Soratobu Toshikeikaku (page does not exist)")* (*[A Flying City Plan](/w/index.php?title=A_Flying_City_Plan&action=edit&redlink=1 "A Flying City Plan (page does not exist)")*) | Yoshiyuki Momose |  | A music video created by Studio Kajino for Capsule |
| *[Looking For A Home](/wiki/Short_films_by_Studio_Ghibli#Looking_for_a_Home "Short films by Studio Ghibli")* | Hayao Miyazaki | | Ghibli Museum |
| 2006 | *[Hoshi o Katta Hi](/wiki/Hoshi_o_Katta_Hi "Hoshi o Katta Hi")* (*[The Day I Raised a Planet](/w/index.php?title=The_Day_I_Raised_a_Planet&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Day I Raised a Planet (page does not exist)")*/*[The Day I Harvested a Planet](/wiki/The_Day_I_Harvested_a_Planet "The Day I Harvested a Planet")*) |
| *[Water Spider Monmon](/wiki/Water_Spider_Monmon "Water Spider Monmon")* |
| *[The Night of Taneyamagahara](/wiki/The_Night_of_Taneyamagahara "The Night of Taneyamagahara")* | [Kazuo Oga](/wiki/Kazuo_Oga "Kazuo Oga") |  | A DVD version was released for Japan on July 7, 2006 |
| 2007 | *[Iblard Jikan](/wiki/Iblard_Jikan "Iblard Jikan")* | [Naohisa Inoue](/wiki/Naohisa_Inoue "Naohisa Inoue") |  | Released in Japan on DVD and Blu-ray disc on July 4, 2007, as part of the Ghibli ga Ippai Collection |
| 2009 | "[Piece](/wiki/Piece_(song) "Piece (song)")" | Yoshiyuki Momose |  | A promotional music video for [Yui Aragaki](/wiki/Yui_Aragaki "Yui Aragaki") |
| 2010 | *[A Sumo Wrestler's Tail](/wiki/A_Sumo_Wrestler%27s_Tail "A Sumo Wrestler's Tail")* | [Akihiko Yamashita](/wiki/Akihiko_Yamashita "Akihiko Yamashita") |  | Ghibli Museum |

The production of this short, which began secretly in 2016, was covered in the NHK documentary [Never-Ending Man: Hayao Miyazaki](/wiki/Never-Ending_Man:_Hayao_Miyazaki "Never-Ending Man: Hayao Miyazaki") (2017) |

This is a list of works (films, television, shorts etc.) by the Japanese [animation studio](/wiki/Animation_studio "Animation studio") [Studio Ghibli](/wiki/Studio_Ghibli "Studio Ghibli").
Works
-----
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_Studio_Ghibli_works&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Works")]
### Feature films
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_Studio_Ghibli_works&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Feature films")]
| Year | English Title | Japanese Title | Director | Screenwriter(s) | Producer(s) | Music | Commercial premiere | Running time |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1986 | *[Castle in the Sky](/wiki/Castle_in_the_Sky "Castle in the Sky")* | 天空の城ラピュタ | [Hayao Miyazaki](/wiki/Hayao_Miyazaki "Hayao Miyazaki") | | [Isao Takahata](/wiki/Isao_Takahata "Isao Takahata") | [Joe Hisaishi](/wiki/Joe_Hisaishi "Joe Hisaishi") | Japan: August 2, 1986 | 125 minutes |
| 1988 | *[My Neighbor Totoro](/wiki/My_Neighbor_Totoro "My Neighbor Totoro")* | となりのトトロ | Toru Hara | Japan: April 16, 1988 | 86 minutes |
| *[Grave of the Fireflies](/wiki/Grave_of_the_Fireflies "Grave of the Fireflies")* | 火垂るの墓 | Isao Takahata | | Michio Mamiya | 88 minutes |
| 1989 | *[Kiki's Delivery Service](/wiki/Kiki%27s_Delivery_Service "Kiki's Delivery Service")* | 魔女の宅急便 | Hayao Miyazaki | | | Joe Hisaishi | Japan: July 29, 1989 | 103 minutes |
| 1991 | *[Only Yesterday](/wiki/Only_Yesterday_(1991_film) "Only Yesterday (1991 film)")* | おもひでぽろぽろ | Isao Takahata | | [Toshio Suzuki](/wiki/Toshio_Suzuki_(producer) "Toshio Suzuki (producer)") | Masaru Hoshi | Japan: July 20, 1991 English dub premiere: January 1, 2016 | 119 minutes |
| 1992 | *[Porco Rosso](/wiki/Porco_Rosso "Porco Rosso")* | 紅の豚 | Hayao Miyazaki | | Joe Hisaishi | Japan: July 18, 1992 | 93 minutes |
| 1994 | *[Pom Poko](/wiki/Pom_Poko "Pom Poko")* | 平成狸合戦ぽんぽこ | Isao Takahata | | [Shang Shang Typhoon](/wiki/Shang_Shang_Typhoon "Shang Shang Typhoon") | Japan: July 16, 1994 | 119 minutes |

The **Grammy Award for Album of the Year** is an award presented by the [National Academy of Recording Arts and Sciences](/wiki/The_Recording_Academy "The Recording Academy") of the United States to "honor artistic achievement, technical proficiency and overall excellence in the recording industry, without regard to [album](/wiki/Album "Album") sales, chart position, or critical reception."[[1]](#cite_note-Overview-1) Album of the Year is the most prestigious category at the [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards") and is one of the general field categories that have been presented annually since the [1st Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/1st_Annual_Grammy_Awards "1st Annual Grammy Awards") in 1959 alongside [Best New Artist](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_New_Artist "Grammy Award for Best New Artist"), [Record of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Record_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Record of the Year"), and [Song of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Song_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Song of the Year").
Credit rules
------------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grammy_Award_for_Album_of_the_Year&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Credit rules")]
Over the years, the rules on who was presented with an award have changed:
* 1959–1965: Artist only.
* 1966–1998: Artist and producer.
* 1999–2002: Artist, producer, and recording engineer or mixer.
* 2003–2017: Artist, featured artist, producer, mastering engineer, and recording engineer or mixer.
* 2018–2020: Artist, featured artist, producer, songwriter (of new material), mastering engineer, and recording engineer or mixer (only those who were credited on at least 33% playing time of the album)
* 2021–2023: Artist, featured artist, producer, songwriter (of new material), mastering engineer, and recording engineer or mixer (regardless of credited playing time)
* 2024–future: Artist, featured artist, producer, songwriter (of new material), mastering engineer, and recording engineer or mixer (only those who were credited on at least 20% playing time of the album)
The category expanded to include eight nominees in [2019](/wiki/61st_Annual_Grammy_Awards "61st Annual Grammy Awards")[[2]](#cite_note-2) and ten in [2022](/wiki/64th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "64th Annual Grammy Awards").[[3]](#cite_note-3) Beginning with the [2024 ceremony](/wiki/66th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "66th Annual Grammy Awards"), the number of nominees has been reduced back to eight.[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4)
Album of the Year is awarded for a whole album, and the award is presented to the artist, featured artist, producer, songwriter, mastering engineer, and recording engineer or mixer with significant contributions to that album. The similarly titled Record of the Year is awarded for a single or for one track from an album. This award goes to the artist, producer, mastering engineer, and recording engineer or mixer for that song.[[5]](#cite_note-5)
Achievements
------------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grammy_Award_for_Album_of_the_Year&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Achievements")]
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/191125_Taylor_Swift_at_the_2019_American_Music_Awards.png/250px-191125_Taylor_Swift_at_the_2019_American_Music_Awards.png)](/wiki/File:191125_Taylor_Swift_at_the_2019_American_Music_Awards.png)
Four-time winner [Taylor Swift](/wiki/Taylor_Swift "Taylor Swift"). She is the first woman to win the award two and three times and the only main credited artist to win four times. She won in [2010](/wiki/52nd_Annual_Grammy_Awards "52nd Annual Grammy Awards"), [2016](/wiki/58th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "58th Annual Grammy Awards"), [2021](/wiki/63rd_Annual_Grammy_Awards "63rd Annual Grammy Awards") and [2024](/wiki/66th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "66th Annual Grammy Awards"). She is the most nominated female act with 7 nominations.
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Frank_Sinatra_%281957_studio_portrait_close-up%29.jpg/250px-Frank_Sinatra_%281957_studio_portrait_close-up%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Sinatra_(1957_studio_portrait_close-up).jpg)
[Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra "Frank Sinatra") was the first two-time winner and three-time winner. He won in [1960](/wiki/2nd_Annual_Grammy_Awards "2nd Annual Grammy Awards"), [1966](/wiki/8th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "8th Annual Grammy Awards") and [1967](/wiki/9th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "9th Annual Grammy Awards"). He is the most nominated artist in this category with 8 nominations.
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Stevie_Wonder_1973.JPG/250px-Stevie_Wonder_1973.JPG)](/wiki/File:Stevie_Wonder_1973.JPG)
Three-time winner [Stevie Wonder](/wiki/Stevie_Wonder "Stevie Wonder") won in [1974](/wiki/16th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "16th Annual Grammy Awards"), [1975](/wiki/17th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "17th Annual Grammy Awards") and [1977](/wiki/19th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "19th Annual Grammy Awards").
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Paul_Simon_in_1982.jpg/250px-Paul_Simon_in_1982.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paul_Simon_in_1982.jpg)
Three-time winner [Paul Simon](/wiki/Paul_Simon "Paul Simon") won twice as the main credited artist, in [1976](/wiki/18th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "18th Annual Grammy Awards") and [1987](/wiki/29th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "29th Annual Grammy Awards").
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/U2_on_Joshua_Tree_Tour_2017_Brussels_8-1-17.jpg/250px-U2_on_Joshua_Tree_Tour_2017_Brussels_8-1-17.jpg)](/wiki/File:U2_on_Joshua_Tree_Tour_2017_Brussels_8-1-17.jpg)
[U2](/wiki/U2 "U2") is the only group act to win twice, in [1988](/wiki/30th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "30th Annual Grammy Awards") and [2006](/wiki/48th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "48th Annual Grammy Awards").
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Adele_-_Live_2016%2C_Glasgow_SSE_Hydro_03.jpg/250px-Adele_-_Live_2016%2C_Glasgow_SSE_Hydro_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adele_-_Live_2016,_Glasgow_SSE_Hydro_03.jpg)
Two-time winner [Adele](/wiki/Adele "Adele") won in [2012](/wiki/54th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "54th Annual Grammy Awards") and [2017](/wiki/59th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "59th Annual Grammy Awards").
[Serban Ghenea](/wiki/Serban_Ghenea "Serban Ghenea") (as engineer/mixer) is the most frequent winner in this category with five awards. [Taylor Swift](/wiki/Taylor_Swift "Taylor Swift") (as performer); [John Hanes](/wiki/John_Hanes_(audio_engineer) "John Hanes (audio engineer)") (as engineer/mixer); and [Tom Coyne](/wiki/Tom_Coyne_(music_engineer) "Tom Coyne (music engineer)") and [Randy Merrill](/wiki/Randy_Merrill "Randy Merrill") (as mastering engineers) have won the award four times.[[6]](#cite_note-6) They are followed by [Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra "Frank Sinatra"), [Stevie Wonder](/wiki/Stevie_Wonder "Stevie Wonder"), and [Paul Simon](/wiki/Paul_Simon "Paul Simon") (as performers); [Jack Antonoff](/wiki/Jack_Antonoff "Jack Antonoff"), [David Foster](/wiki/David_Foster "David Foster"), [Daniel Lanois](/wiki/Daniel_Lanois "Daniel Lanois"), [Phil Ramone](/wiki/Phil_Ramone "Phil Ramone"), and [Ryan Tedder](/wiki/Ryan_Tedder "Ryan Tedder") (as album producers); [Tom Elmhirst](/wiki/Tom_Elmhirst "Tom Elmhirst") and [Mike Piersante](/wiki/Mike_Piersante "Mike Piersante") and Laura Sisk (as engineers/mixers); and [Bob Ludwig](/wiki/Bob_Ludwig "Bob Ludwig") (as mastering engineer) with three victories each. Coyne, Ghenea, Hanes, and Ludwig are the only people to win the award in three consecutive years. [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney") leads all performers with nine nominations: five as a member of [The Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles"), three for solo albums, and one as a member of [Wings](/wiki/Wings_(band) "Wings (band)"). McCartney's former bandmate [George Harrison](/wiki/George_Harrison "George Harrison") has a total of eight nominations: five as a member of The Beatles, one for a solo album, one for a live album with friends, and one as a member of the [Traveling Wilburys](/wiki/Traveling_Wilburys "Traveling Wilburys"). Sinatra leads solo performers with eight nominations: seven for solo albums and one for a duet album. [Taylor Swift](/wiki/Taylor_Swift "Taylor Swift") has the most nominations amongst female artists with seven, followed by [Barbra Streisand](/wiki/Barbra_Streisand "Barbra Streisand") with six.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)
The first woman to win the award was [Judy Garland](/wiki/Judy_Garland "Judy Garland") in 1962, for *[Judy at Carnegie Hall](/wiki/Judy_at_Carnegie_Hall "Judy at Carnegie Hall")*. Taylor Swift is the first solo female artist to win the award two, three, and four times. [Adele](/wiki/Adele "Adele") has won twice as main credited artist, and [Lauryn Hill](/wiki/Lauryn_Hill "Lauryn Hill"), [Norah Jones](/wiki/Norah_Jones "Norah Jones"), and [Alison Krauss](/wiki/Alison_Krauss "Alison Krauss") also won two times each, first as lead artists for their respective albums, *[The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill](/wiki/The_Miseducation_of_Lauryn_Hill "The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill")*, *[Come Away with Me](/wiki/Come_Away_with_Me "Come Away with Me")*, and *[Raising Sand](/wiki/Raising_Sand "Raising Sand")* (Krauss' collaboration album with [Robert Plant](/wiki/Robert_Plant "Robert Plant")); Hill won her second Album of the Year as a producer of her collaboration on [Santana](/wiki/Santana_(band) "Santana (band)")'s *[Supernatural](/wiki/Supernatural_(Santana_album) "Supernatural (Santana album)")* (featured artists on non-soundtrack albums did not share in the award before 2008), while Jones won again as a featured artist on [Herbie Hancock](/wiki/Herbie_Hancock "Herbie Hancock")'s *[River: The Joni Letters](/wiki/River:_The_Joni_Letters "River: The Joni Letters")*, and Krauss won again having been a featured artist on the *[O Brother, Where Art Thou? – Soundtrack](/wiki/O_Brother,_Where_Art_Thou%3F_(soundtrack) "O Brother, Where Art Thou? (soundtrack)")*.
[Billie Eilish](/wiki/Billie_Eilish "Billie Eilish") is the youngest main credit artist to win in the category, winning for her debut album *When We All Fall Asleep, Where Do We Go?* in 2020 at the age of 18. [The Peasall Sisters](/wiki/The_Peasall_Sisters "The Peasall Sisters"), Sarah, Hannah, and Leah, are the category's youngest credited winners, winning for their contributions to the *O Brother, Where Art Thou? – Soundtrack* at ages 13, 9, and 7 respectively. Leah Peasall is the youngest winner of any Grammy in any category.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The youngest person to make an appearance on an Album of the Year is Stevie Wonder's daughter Aisha Morris, who appeared as an infant for "[Isn't She Lovely?](/wiki/Isn%27t_She_Lovely%3F "Isn't She Lovely?")" on the album *[Songs in the Key of Life](/wiki/Songs_in_the_Key_of_Life "Songs in the Key of Life")*.[[10]](#cite_note-10)
[Christopher Cross](/wiki/Christopher_Cross "Christopher Cross") and Billie Eilish are the only artists to receive Grammys for Album of the Year as well as [Record of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Record_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Record of the Year"), [Song of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Song_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Song of the Year"), and [Best New Artist](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_New_Artist "Grammy Award for Best New Artist"), each winning all four "general field" categories in a single ceremony year. Adele was the first artist to win awards for Album of the Year, Record of the Year, Song of the Year, and Best New Artist on separate occasions. Five artists have won both Album of the Year and Best New Artist in the same year: [Bob Newhart](/wiki/Bob_Newhart "Bob Newhart") (*[The Button-Down Mind of Bob Newhart](/wiki/The_Button-Down_Mind_of_Bob_Newhart "The Button-Down Mind of Bob Newhart")* in 1961), Lauryn Hill (*The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill* in 1999), and Norah Jones (*Come Away with Me* in 2003) along with Christopher Cross (*[Christopher Cross](/wiki/Christopher_Cross_(album) "Christopher Cross (album)")* in 1981) and Billie Eilish (*When We All Fall Asleep, Where Do We Go?* in 2020) mentioned above.
Frank Sinatra's *[Come Dance with Me!](/wiki/Come_Dance_with_Me!_(album) "Come Dance with Me! (album)")* was the first album by a [traditional pop](/wiki/Traditional_pop "Traditional pop") artist to win, [

question: How many Kannada films have grossed at least ₹100 crore worldwide and also earned at least ₹50 crore during their opening week?

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
[Kannada cinema](/wiki/Kannada_cinema "Kannada cinema") is a part of [Indian cinema](/wiki/Cinema_of_India "Cinema of India") based in [Bengaluru](/wiki/Bengaluru "Bengaluru"), [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka"). The films are made primarily in [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada "Kannada")-[language](/wiki/Language "Language"). The 2006 film *[Mungaru Male](/wiki/Mungaru_Male "Mungaru Male")* is the first Kannada movie to gross ₹50 crores.[[1]](#cite_note-MungaruMale-1) The 2017 film *[Raajakumara](/wiki/Raajakumara "Raajakumara")* is the first Kannada movie to gross ₹75 crore mark.[[2]](#cite_note-Raajakumara-2)
*[KGF: Chapter 1](/wiki/KGF:_Chapter_1 "KGF: Chapter 1")* (2018) is the first Kannada film to achieve ₹100, ₹150, ₹200 and ₹250 crore gross milestone.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Its sequel *[KGF: Chapter 2](/wiki/KGF:_Chapter_2 "KGF: Chapter 2")* (2022) is the first Kannada film to achieve ₹500, ₹750, ₹1,000 and ₹1,250 crore gross milestones.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-Pinkvilla6March2023-8)[[9]](#cite_note-newsable.asianetnews.com-9)
*[Kantara: Chapter 1](/wiki/Kantara:_Chapter_1 "Kantara: Chapter 1")* (2025) was reported to be the first film to gross over ₹200 crore as well as ₹250 crore within Karnataka.[[10]](#cite_note-10) This ranking lists the highest-grossing Kannada films which are filmed in Kannada language, based on conservative global [box office](/wiki/Box_office "Box office") estimates as reported by organisations classified as green by Wikipedia.[[a]](#cite_note-11) However, there is no official tracking of figures.

----------------------

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| 2 | *[Kantara: Chapter 1](/wiki/Kantara:_Chapter_1 "Kantara: Chapter 1")* | ₹850–900 crore | 2025 | [[d]](#cite_note-kc1-34) |

| 4 | *[Mahavatar Narsimha](/wiki/Mahavatar_Narsimha "Mahavatar Narsimha")*[[f]](#cite_note-language-46) | ₹300–325 crore | 2025 | [[g]](#cite_note-52) |

| 7 | *[James](/wiki/James_(2022_film) "James (2022 film)")* | ₹151 crore | [[14]](#cite_note-HindustanTimes15October2022-15) |

**Films excluded from the list:**
* In case of *[Kaatera](/wiki/Kaatera "Kaatera")* (2023), the box-office collection range is considered from ₹67 crores to ₹200 crores due to wide variation in the box-office figures reported by various sources. Since the 10th ranked film has a gross in excess of the lower end range of ₹67 crores considered for *Kaatera*, the same has been excluded from the list. *[Bollywood Hungama](/wiki/Bollywood_Hungama "Bollywood Hungama")*  gave a domestic gross figure of ₹67 crores.[[57]](#cite_note-:KAT67-67) *[Deccan Herald](/wiki/Deccan_Herald "Deccan Herald")* reported its collections to be less than ₹90 crores.[[58]](#cite_note-Jayaraman-68) *[The Hindu](/wiki/The_Hindu "The Hindu")* and *[Zee Media Corporation](/wiki/Zee_Media_Corporation "Zee Media Corporation")* reported the collections to be over ₹100 crores while other websites reported the collections to be ₹200 crores amidst speculation of tampering box office reports by *[HT Media](/wiki/HT_Media "HT Media")*.[[59]](#cite_note-The_Hindu-69)[[60]](#cite_note-ZEE5_Blog-70)[[61]](#cite_note-Math200-71)[[62]](#cite_note-Darshan's_'Kaatera'_gets_digital_re-72)[[63]](#cite_note-Features-73)[[64]](#cite_note-Entertainment_Bureau-74)
* *[Roberrt](/wiki/Roberrt "Roberrt")* (2021) is excluded from the list because three years after its theatrical run, the producer admitted to have fabricated its collections and had inflated the figures earlier;[[65]](#cite_note-timesofindia.indiatimes.com-75) revealing the actual collection to be only ₹55 crores as against the trade estimate of 78 crores at the time of its release.[[66]](#cite_note-ROB55-76)[[67]](#cite_note-ANITHA_E-77)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
[Kannada cinema](/wiki/Kannada_cinema "Kannada cinema") is a part of [Indian cinema](/wiki/Cinema_of_India "Cinema of India") based in [Bengaluru](/wiki/Bengaluru "Bengaluru"), [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka"). The films are made primarily in [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada "Kannada")-[language](/wiki/Language "Language"). The 2006 film *[Mungaru Male](/wiki/Mungaru_Male "Mungaru Male")* is the first Kannada movie to gross ₹50 crores.[[1]](#cite_note-MungaruMale-1) The 2017 film *[Raajakumara](/wiki/Raajakumara "Raajakumara")* is the first Kannada movie to gross ₹75 crore mark.[[2]](#cite_note-Raajakumara-2)
*[KGF: Chapter 1](/wiki/KGF:_Chapter_1 "KGF: Chapter 1")* (2018) is the first Kannada film to achieve ₹100, ₹150, ₹200 and ₹250 crore gross milestone.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Its sequel *[KGF: Chapter 2](/wiki/KGF:_Chapter_2 "KGF: Chapter 2")* (2022) is the first Kannada film to achieve ₹500, ₹750, ₹1,000 and ₹1,250 crore gross milestones.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-Pinkvilla6March2023-8)[[9]](#cite_note-newsable.asianetnews.com-9)
*[Kantara: Chapter 1](/wiki/Kantara:_Chapter_1 "Kantara: Chapter 1")* (2025) was reported to be the first film to gross over ₹200 crore as well as ₹250 crore within Karnataka.[[10]](#cite_note-10) This ranking lists the highest-grossing Kannada films which are filmed in Kannada language, based on conservative global [box office](/wiki/Box_office "Box office") estimates as reported by organisations classified as green by Wikipedia.[[a]](#cite_note-11) However, there is no official tracking of figures.

**Films excluded from the list:**
* In case of *[Kaatera](/wiki/Kaatera "Kaatera")* (2023), the box-office collection range is considered from ₹67 crores to ₹200 crores due to wide variation in the box-office figures reported by various sources. Since the 10th ranked film has a gross in excess of the lower end range of ₹67 crores considered for *Kaatera*, the same has been excluded from the list. *[Bollywood Hungama](/wiki/Bollywood_Hungama "Bollywood Hungama")*  gave a domestic gross figure of ₹67 crores.[[57]](#cite_note-:KAT67-67) *[Deccan Herald](/wiki/Deccan_Herald "Deccan Herald")* reported its collections to be less than ₹90 crores.[[58]](#cite_note-Jayaraman-68) *[The Hindu](/wiki/The_Hindu "The Hindu")* and *[Zee Media Corporation](/wiki/Zee_Media_Corporation "Zee Media Corporation")* reported the collections to be over ₹100 crores while other websites reported the collections to be ₹200 crores amidst speculation of tampering box office reports by *[HT Media](/wiki/HT_Media "HT Media")*.[[59]](#cite_note-The_Hindu-69)[[60]](#cite_note-ZEE5_Blog-70)[[61]](#cite_note-Math200-71)[[62]](#cite_note-Darshan's_'Kaatera'_gets_digital_re-72)[[63]](#cite_note-Features-73)[[64]](#cite_note-Entertainment_Bureau-74)
* *[Roberrt](/wiki/Roberrt "Roberrt")* (2021) is excluded from the list because three years after its theatrical run, the producer admitted to have fabricated its collections and had inflated the figures earlier;[[65]](#cite_note-timesofindia.indiatimes.com-75) revealing the actual collection to be only ₹55 crores as against the trade estimate of 78 crores at the time of its release.[[66]](#cite_note-ROB55-76)[[67]](#cite_note-ANITHA_E-77)

| Disease | [Mpox](/wiki/Mpox "Mpox") (clade Ib) |
| Location | Primarily [Central Africa](/wiki/Central_Africa "Central Africa"), [limited local transmission](#Countries_with_limited_local_transmission) elsewhere |
| Date | First detected September 2023  ---  Declared [public health emergency of international concern](/wiki/Public_health_emergency_of_international_concern "Public health emergency of international concern"): 14 August 2024-5 September 2025 |
| Confirmed cases | 54,116 since 1 Jan 2024[[1]](#cite_note-2025Dec18WHO-1) |
| Deaths | 492 since 1 Jan 2022[[1]](#cite_note-2025Dec18WHO-1) |
An [epidemic](/wiki/Epidemic "Epidemic") of a new variant of [clade](/wiki/Clade "Clade") I [mpox](/wiki/Mpox "Mpox") (formerly known as monkeypox), called clade 1b,[[2]](#cite_note-World-Health-Organization-2024-2) began in [Central Africa](/wiki/Central_Africa "Central Africa") at least as early as September 2023.[[3]](#cite_note-Cheng-2024-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Junaidi1-2024-4) As of September 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=2023%E2%80%932026_mpox_epidemic&action=edit), more than 29,000 cases have been reported, with over 800 fatalities (~3% [fatality rate](/wiki/Case_fatality_rate "Case fatality rate")),[[5]](#cite_note-Reuters20240923-5) nearly all in the [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Mpox_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Mpox in the Democratic Republic of the Congo").[[6]](#cite_note-Cheng-2024a-6) According to the [European CDC](/wiki/European_Centre_for_Disease_Prevention_and_Control "European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control"): "The size of these outbreaks could be larger than reported due to under-ascertainment and under-reporting."[[7]](#cite_note-7)
On 14 August 2024, the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") declared the epidemic a [public health emergency of international concern](/wiki/Public_health_emergency_of_international_concern "Public health emergency of international concern").[[2]](#cite_note-World-Health-Organization-2024-2)[[8]](#cite_note-Mandavilli-2024-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:7-9)
Background
----------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2023%E2%80%932026_mpox_epidemic&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]
Main article: [2022–2023 mpox outbreak](/wiki/2022%E2%80%932023_mpox_outbreak "2022–2023 mpox outbreak")
Mpox is an infectious [viral disease](/wiki/Viral_disease "Viral disease") that is endemic among small mammals in central areas of Africa; it can also infect humans.[[10]](#cite_note-WHOfact2023-10) Symptoms include a [rash](/wiki/Rash "Rash") that forms [blisters](/wiki/Blister "Blister") and then crusts over, fever, and [swollen lymph nodes](/wiki/Lymphadenopathy "Lymphadenopathy").[[10]](#cite_note-WHOfact2023-10) The illness is usually mild, and most infected individuals recover within a few weeks without treatment.[[11]](#cite_note-Gov.UK2022-11) However, cases may be severe, especially in children, pregnant women, or people with suppressed immune systems.[[12]](#cite_note-WHO_Q&A-12)
In May 2022, the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") (WHO) declared a prior [mpox outbreak](/wiki/2022%E2%80%932023_mpox_outbreak "2022–2023 mpox outbreak"), involving a clade IIb of the virus, a [global health emergency](/wiki/Global_health_emergency "Global health emergency"). The disease had infected 87,000 individuals and caused 140 deaths when the WHO ended its global emergency in May 2023, citing a combination of vaccination and public health information as successful control measures.[[13]](#cite_note-WHO2023-05-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) Cases of clade IIb which can be traced to this outbreak are continuing to be detected worldwide, although at a relatively low level.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
In September 2023, cases of mpox started increasing in the [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo") and continued into 2024. The [Africa Centres for Disease Control and Prevention](/wiki/Africa_Centres_for_Disease_Control_and_Prevention "Africa Centres for Disease Control and Prevention") (ACDC) reported a 160% increase in cases from the previous year. Most of these cases were determined to be caused by clade I of the virus.[[3]](#cite_note-Cheng-2024-3)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
.mw-parser-output .hatnote{font-style:italic}.mw-parser-output div.hatnote{padding-left:1.6em;margin-bottom:0.5em}.mw-parser-output .hatnote i{font-style:normal}.mw-parser-output .hatnote+link+.hatnote{margin-top:-0.5em}@media print{body.ns-0 .mw-parser-output .hatnote{display:none!important}}
This is a [dynamic list](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Lists#Dynamic_lists "Wikipedia:WikiProject Lists") and may never be able to satisfy particular standards for completeness. You can help by [editing the page](/wiki/Special:EditPage/List_of_most-liked_Instagram_posts "Special:EditPage/List of most-liked Instagram posts") to add missing items, with references to [reliable sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources").
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Lionel_Messi_WC2022.jpg/250px-Lionel_Messi_WC2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lionel_Messi_WC2022.jpg)
[Lionel Messi](/wiki/Lionel_Messi "Lionel Messi"), the current record-holder, at the [2022 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup "2022 FIFA World Cup").
This **list of most-liked Instagram posts** contains the top 20 posts by number of [likes](/wiki/Like_button#Instagram "Like button") on the photo and video-sharing social networking service [Instagram](/wiki/Instagram "Instagram").
The most-liked post as of April 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=List_of_most-liked_Instagram_posts&action=edit) is of the Argentine [footballer](/wiki/Football_player "Football player") [Lionel Messi](/wiki/Lionel_Messi "Lionel Messi") and his teammates celebrating the [2022 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup "2022 FIFA World Cup") win at [Lusail Stadium](/wiki/Lusail_Stadium "Lusail Stadium") in [Lusail](/wiki/Lusail "Lusail"), [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar "Qatar"), which has been liked by over 74 million different accounts. Uploaded on December 18, 2022, the post shows Messi lifting his first World Cup Trophy after [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_national_football_team "Argentina national football team")'s win over [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team") in the [final](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup_final "2022 FIFA World Cup final"). It is also the most liked post of all time across all social media platforms.[[1]](#cite_note-Corbella-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Navlakha-2)
History
-------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_most-liked_Instagram_posts&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]
On January 4, 2019, the account [@world\_record\_egg](/wiki/Instagram_egg "Instagram egg") posted a photo of an egg with the specific purpose of surpassing the then most-liked Instagram post, a picture of [Kylie Jenner](/wiki/Kylie_Jenner "Kylie Jenner")'s daughter with 18.6 million likes.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The photo of the egg was originally taken by Serghei Platanov, who then posted it to [Shutterstock](/wiki/Shutterstock "Shutterstock") on June 23, 2015, with the title "eggs isolated on white background."[[4]](#cite_note-INSIDER-4) The creator of the @world\_record\_egg account was Chris Godfrey, an advertising creative, and his friends CJ Brown and Alissa Khan-Whelan.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)
On January 14, 2019, the egg post became the most-liked post on Instagram, to which the egg's account owner wrote, "This is madness. What a time to be alive."[[7]](#cite_note-7) Jenner responded with a video on Instagram of her cracking an egg open with the caption: "Take that little egg."[[8]](#cite_note-8) Platanov was surprised by his photo's popularity, writing, "Egg is just an egg."[[4]](#cite_note-INSIDER-4)
On December 18, 2022, [footballer](/wiki/Football_player "Football player") [Lionel Messi](/wiki/Lionel_Messi "Lionel Messi") posted a [carousel](/wiki/Instagram#Features_and_tools "Instagram") with photos of him lifting the [FIFA World Cup Trophy](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup_Trophy "FIFA World Cup Trophy") and celebrating with his teammates after winning the [2022 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup "2022 FIFA World Cup") with [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_national_football_team "Argentina national football team"), which reached 10 million likes within the first 39 minutes of its sharing.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The following day, the post became the most-liked Instagram post in the first 24 hours of its sharing, with 50 million likes.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) On December 20, 2022, the post reached over 56 million likes, surpassing the previous record held by [@world\_record\_egg](/wiki/Instagram_egg "Instagram egg").[[12]](#cite_note-messisurpass-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)
Within 48 hours of Messi posting, the [Instagram](/wiki/Instagram "Instagram") post had surpassed 64 million likes, and in doing so, it became the most-liked social media post ever across all social media platforms, by overtaking the most-liked post on [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube"), the music video for the song "[Despacito](/wiki/Despacito "Despacito")", which had reached 50.2 million likes, and the most-liked post on [TikTok](/wiki/TikTok "TikTok"), a video uploaded by [Bella Poarch](/wiki/Bella_Poarch "Bella Poarch") featuring her lip-syncing to the song "Sophie Aspin Send", which had reached 60.3 million likes.[[1]](#cite_note-Corbella-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Navlakha-2) This also meant that it became the first ever post on Instagram to reach 60 and 70 million likes, achieving that in just 3 days since it was posted.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
Top 20
------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_most-liked_Instagram_posts&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Top 20")]
[Lionel Messi](/wiki/Lionel_Messi "Lionel Messi") makes 6 while [Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo") makes 5 out of the top 20 most-liked posts.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
.mw-parser-output .hatnote{font-style:italic}.mw-parser-output div.hatnote{padding-left:1.6em;margin-bottom:0.5em}.mw-parser-output .hatnote i{font-style:normal}.mw-parser-output .hatnote+link+.hatnote{margin-top:-0.5em}@media print{body.ns-0 .mw-parser-output .hatnote{display:none!important}}
This is a [dynamic list](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Lists#Dynamic_lists "Wikipedia:WikiProject Lists") and may never be able to satisfy particular standards for completeness. You can help by [editing the page](/wiki/Special:EditPage/List_of_most-liked_Instagram_posts "Special:EditPage/List of most-liked Instagram posts") to add missing items, with references to [reliable sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources").
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Lionel_Messi_WC2022.jpg/250px-Lionel_Messi_WC2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lionel_Messi_WC2022.jpg)
[Lionel Messi](/wiki/Lionel_Messi "Lionel Messi"), the current record-holder, at the [2022 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup "2022 FIFA World Cup").
This **list of most-liked Instagram posts** contains the top 20 posts by number of [likes](/wiki/Like_button#Instagram "Like button") on the photo and video-sharing social networking service [Instagram](/wiki/Instagram "Instagram").
The most-liked post as of April 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=List_of_most-liked_Instagram_posts&action=edit) is of the Argentine [footballer](/wiki/Football_player "Football player") [Lionel Messi](/wiki/Lionel_Messi "Lionel Messi") and his teammates celebrating the [2022 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup "2022 FIFA World Cup") win at [Lusail Stadium](/wiki/Lusail_Stadium "Lusail Stadium") in [Lusail](/wiki/Lusail "Lusail"), [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar "Qatar"), which has been liked by over 74 million different accounts. Uploaded on December 18, 2022, the post shows Messi lifting his first World Cup Trophy after [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_national_football_team "Argentina national football team")'s win over [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team") in the [final](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup_final "2022 FIFA World Cup final"). It is also the most liked post of all time across all social media platforms.[[1]](#cite_note-Corbella-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Navlakha-2)
History
-------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_most-liked_Instagram_posts&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]
On January 4, 2019, the account [@world\_record\_egg](/wiki/Instagram_egg "Instagram egg") posted a photo of an egg with the specific purpose of surpassing the then most-liked Instagram post, a picture of [Kylie Jenner](/wiki/Kylie_Jenner "Kylie Jenner")'s daughter with 18.6 million likes.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The photo of the egg was originally taken by Serghei Platanov, who then posted it to [Shutterstock](/wiki/Shutterstock "Shutterstock") on June 23, 2015, with the title "eggs isolated on white background."[[4]](#cite_note-INSIDER-4) The creator of the @world\_record\_egg account was Chris Godfrey, an advertising creative, and his friends CJ Brown and Alissa Khan-Whelan.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)
On January 14, 2019, the egg post became the most-liked post on Instagram, to which the egg's account owner wrote, "This is madness. What a time to be alive."[[7]](#cite_note-7) Jenner responded with a video on Instagram of her cracking an egg open with the caption: "Take that little egg."[[8]](#cite_note-8) Platanov was surprised by his photo's popularity, writing, "Egg is just an egg."[[4]](#cite_note-INSIDER-4)
On December 18, 2022, [footballer](/wiki/Football_player "Football player") [Lionel Messi](/wiki/Lionel_Messi "Lionel Messi") posted a [carousel](/wiki/Instagram#Features_and_tools "Instagram") with photos of him lifting the [FIFA World Cup Trophy](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup_Trophy "FIFA World Cup Trophy") and celebrating with his teammates after winning the [2022 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup "2022 FIFA World Cup") with [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_national_football_team "Argentina national football team"), which reached 10 million likes within the first 39 minutes of its sharing.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The following day, the post became the most-liked Instagram post in the first 24 hours of its sharing, with 50 million likes.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) On December 20, 2022, the post reached over 56 million likes, surpassing the previous record held by [@world\_record\_egg](/wiki/Instagram_egg "Instagram egg").[[12]](#cite_note-messisurpass-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)
Within 48 hours of Messi posting, the [Instagram](/wiki/Instagram "Instagram") post had surpassed 64 million likes, and in doing so, it became the most-liked social media post ever across all social media platforms, by overtaking the most-liked post on [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube"), the music video for the song "[Despacito](/wiki/Despacito "Despacito")", which had reached 50.2 million likes, and the most-liked post on [TikTok](/wiki/TikTok "TikTok"), a video uploaded by [Bella Poarch](/wiki/Bella_Poarch "Bella Poarch") featuring her lip-syncing to the song "Sophie Aspin Send", which had reached 60.3 million likes.[[1]](#cite_note-Corbella-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Navlakha-2) This also meant that it became the first ever post on Instagram to reach 60 and 70 million likes, achieving that in just 3 days since it was posted.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

| Satellite images of the 15 January 2022 eruption of Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai | |
This is a **list of volcanic eruptions in the 21st century** with a [volcanic explosivity index](/wiki/Volcanic_explosivity_index "Volcanic explosivity index") (VEI) of 4 or higher, and smaller eruptions that resulted in fatalities, significant damage or disruptions.
The largest volcanic eruption of the 21st century is the [2022 Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai eruption and tsunami](/wiki/2022_Hunga_Tonga%E2%80%93Hunga_Ha%CA%BBapai_eruption_and_tsunami "2022 Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai eruption and tsunami"), and the deadliest are the [2018 Volcán de Fuego eruption](/wiki/2018_Volc%C3%A1n_de_Fuego_eruption "2018 Volcán de Fuego eruption") and the [2018 Sunda Strait tsunami](/wiki/2018_Sunda_Strait_tsunami "2018 Sunda Strait tsunami").
Large eruptions (VEI of 4 or higher, or plume height of at least 15 km)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_volcanic_eruptions_in_the_21st_century&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Large eruptions (VEI of 4 or higher, or plume height of at least 15 km)")]
| VEI | Volcano | Country | Year | Max plume height (km) | Material volume (km3) | Fatalities | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2-4? | [Mount Lewotobi](/wiki/Mount_Lewotobi "Mount Lewotobi")[[1]](#cite_note-GVP-Lewotobi-1) | Indonesia | 2024-ongoing | 18 |  | 13 | On 4 November 2024, the volcano spewed molten debris at several villages some 4 km (2.5 mi) away, destroying homes and killing at least nine.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The Centre of Volcanology and Geological Hazard Mitigation recommended that a 7 km (4.3 mi) radius around the volcano be evacuated.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Seven villages were affected by the eruption.[[4]](#cite_note-4) A larger eruption occurred on 7 November.[[5]](#cite_note-5) On 8 November, the volcano erupted several times, one bearing an [ash plume](/wiki/Ash_plume "Ash plume") with a height reaching 10 km (6.2 mi).[[6]](#cite_note-6) On 9 November, it erupted again, scrambling authorities to evacuate approximately 16,000 people from nearby villages.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The volcano erupted twice on 7 July 2025, the first one occurring at 11:00 local time, sending a column of volcanic materials to height up to 18 km (11 mi) which was the volcano's highest since the 7 November 2024 eruption, the second eruption occurred at 19:30 local time, spewing lava and sending clouds of ash up to 13 km (8 mi), at least 24 international flights and 4 domestic flights were cancelled as a result of the eruptions on 7 July 2025.[[8]](#cite_note-8) |
| 4 | [Mount Ruang](/wiki/Mount_Ruang "Mount Ruang")[[9]](#cite_note-GVP-Ruang-9) | Indonesia | 2024 | 23[[10]](#cite_note-10) |  |  | Thousands of homes were destroyed.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Volcanic ash was reported as far away as [Manado](/wiki/Manado "Manado") and several areas in [Gorontalo](/wiki/Gorontalo_(province) "Gorontalo (province)"). Airlines from West Malaysia and Singapore cancelled flights to [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah") and [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak") on 18 April due to reduced visibility.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) The eruption also prompted the shutdown of [Sam Ratulangi International Airport](/wiki/Sam_Ratulangi_International_Airport "Sam Ratulangi International Airport") in [North Sulawesi](/wiki/North_Sulawesi "North Sulawesi").[[14]](#cite_note-14) All 843 residents of Ruang island were evacuated to Manado, while 12,000 residents of Tagulandang were relocated to [Siau Island](/wiki/Siau_Island "Siau Island") by ship.[[15]](#cite_note-15) On 17 and 30 April, authorities raised the volcano's alert level to four, the highest in Indonesia and issued a tsunami alert which led to orders for 11,000 residents and evacuees in Tagulandang to be moved to [Manado](/wiki/Manado "Manado") in mainland [Sulawesi](/wiki/Sulawesi "Sulawesi"), citing the risk of the volcano collapsing into the sea.[[16]](#cite_note-16) |
| 4? | [Sheveluch](/wiki/Sheveluch "Sheveluch")[[17]](#cite_note-GVP-Shiveluch-17) | Russia | 2023 | 20 |  |  | An eruption on 11 April ejected a cloud of volcanic gas and ash that reached a height of 20 km (12 mi) and spread over an area of 108,000 km2 (42,000 sq mi).[[18]](#cite_note-18) [Pyroclastic flows](/wiki/Pyroclastic_flow "Pyroclastic flow") from the eruption traveled up to 19 km (12 mi) away from the volcano.[[19]](#cite_note-gvp11042023-19) |
| 3 | [Bezymianny](/wiki/Bezymianny "Bezymianny")[[20]](#cite_note-GVP-Bezymianny-20) | Russia | 2022 | 15 |  |  | An eruption on 28 May sent ash to an altitude of 15 km, causing some disruptions to flights in the [North Pacific](/wiki/North_Pacific "North Pacific"), including an [American Airlines](/wiki/American_Airlines "American Airlines") flight from [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas") to [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") that diverted back to [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") midway across the Pacific, landing after approximately 12 hours in the air.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] |
| 5-6 | [Hunga Tonga–Hunga Ha'apai](/wiki/Hunga_Tonga%E2%80%93Hunga_Ha%27apai "Hunga Tonga–Hunga Ha'apai")[[21]](#cite_note-GVP-Hunga_Tonga-21) | Tonga | 2022 | 58 | 6–10[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-Tonga_Caldera-24) | 6 | The explosive submarine eruption began on 20 December 2021, with the [largest explosion occurring on January 15, 2022](/wiki/2022_Hunga_Tonga%E2%80%93Hunga_Ha%CA%BBapai_eruption_and_tsunami "2022 Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai eruption and tsunami"). Satellite measurements recorded an eruption column of at least 30,000 m (98,000 ft) into the atmosphere.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The explosion was hundreds of times more powerful than the [atomic bomb dropped on Hiroshima](/wiki/Little_Boy "Little Boy"),[[26]](#cite_note-26) and was heard as far as [Fairbanks](/wiki/Fairbanks "Fairbanks"), [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska"), nearly 10,000 km away. Fluctuations in air pressure were recorded all over the world as the pressure wave fully circled the world several times. Two people were killed in [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru") by a 2-metre tsunami wave. A British woman was found to have been killed by the tsunami in Tonga.[[27]](#cite_note-27) |
| 4 | [Mount Semeru](/wiki/Semeru "Semeru")[[28]](#cite_note-GVP-Mount_Semeru-28) | Indonesia | 2021 | 12 |  | 57 | [An eruption began on December 4](/wiki/2021_Semeru_eruption "2021 Semeru eruption"), and ejected a cloud of volcanic ash 12,000 m (40,000 ft) into the air, killing at least 57 people and injuring more than 100 others. |
| 4 | [Fukutoku-Okanoba](/wiki/Fukutoku-Okanoba "Fukutoku-Okanoba")[[29]](#cite_note-GVP-Fukutoku-Okanoba-29) | Japan | 2021 | 16 |  |  | [Submarine volcano](/wiki/Submarine_volcano "Submarine volcano") approximately 1,300 km south of [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"). In October, a large amount of trachytic composition pumice was seen to have been released in this eruption, and washed ashore on [Okinawa](/wiki/Okinawa_Island "Okinawa Island") and [Amami Islands](/wiki/Amami_Islands "Amami Islands").[[30]](#cite_note-mainichi_2021-10-23-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) |
| 4 | [La Soufrière](/wiki/La_Soufri%C3%A8re_(volcano) "La Soufrière (volcano)")[[33]](#cite_note-GVP-Soufriere-33) | Saint Vincent and the Grenadines | 2021 | 16 | 0.3 |  | [2021 eruption of La Soufrière](/wiki/2021_eruption_of_La_Soufri%C3%A8re "2021 eruption of La Soufrière") |
| 4 | [Taal](/wiki/Taal_Volcano "Taal Volcano")[[34]](#cite_note-GVP-Taal-34) | Philippines | 2020 | 15 |  | 39 | [2020 Taal Volcano eruption](/wiki/2020_Taal_Volcano_eruption "2020 Taal Volcano eruption") A [plinian](/wiki/Plinian "Plinian") eruption from the main crater]] spewed tephra to [Calabarzon](/wiki/Calabarzon "Calabarzon"), [Metro Manila](/wiki/Metro_Manila "Metro Manila"), [Central Luzon](/wiki/Central_Luzon "Central Luzon") and [Pangasinan](/wiki/Pangasinan "Pangasinan"). 39 people were killed.[[35]](#cite_note-35) |
| 4 | [Ulawun](/wiki/Ulawun "Ulawun")[[36]](#cite_note-GVP-Ulawun-36) | Papua New Guinea | 2019 | 19.2 |  |  | On 26 June Ulawun erupted, sending an ash plume to at least 19,000 m (63,000 ft).[[37]](#cite_note-37) Other large eruptions occurred on 2 August, also sending ash to 19,000 m (63,000 ft).[[38]](#cite_note-38) |
| 4 | [Raikoke](/wiki/Raikoke "Raikoke")[[39]](#cite_note-GVP-Raikoke-39) | Russia | 2019 | 17 |  |  | First eruption since 1924. At approximately 4 am, 22 June it erupted, with a plume of ash and gas reaching between 13,000 m (43,000 ft) and 17,000 m (56,000 ft), passing the [tropopause](/wiki/Tropopause "Tropopause") and allowing [stratospheric](/wiki/Stratospheric "Stratospheric") injection of [ash](/wiki/Volcanic_ash "Volcanic ash") and [sulfur dioxide](/wiki/Sulfur_dioxide "Sulfur dioxide").[[40]](#cite_note-40) |
| 3 | [Volcán de Fuego](/wiki/Volc%C3%A1n_de_Fuego "Volcán de Fuego") | Guatemala | 2018 | 15 |  | 190–2,900 | At least 190 people were killed in the [volcano's most powerful eruption since 1974](/wiki/2018_Volc%C3%A1n_de_Fuego_eruption "2018 Volcán de Fuego eruption"). Ash forced the closure of [La Aurora International Airport](/wiki/La_Aurora_International_Airport "La Aurora International Airport") in [Guatemala City](/wiki/Guatemala_City "Guatemala City").[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) |
| 4 | [Volcán Wolf](/wiki/Volc%C3%A1n_Wolf "Volcán Wolf")[[43]](#cite_note-GVP-Wolf-43) | Ecuador | 2015 | 15 |  |  |  |
| 4 | [Calbuco](/wiki/Calbuco_Volcano "Calbuco Volcano")[[44]](#cite_note-GVP-Calbuco-44) | Chile | 2015 | 21 | 0.3–0.6[[45]](#cite_note-45) |  | First eruption since 1972. At least 4,000 people evacuated. No casualties reported.[[46]](#cite_note-46) |
| 4 | [Manam](/wiki/Manam_Motu "Manam Motu")[[47]](#cite_note-GVP-Manam-47) | Papua New Guinea | 2014-ongoing | 19.8 |  |  |  |
| 3 | [Sangeang Api](/wiki/Sangeang_Api "Sangeang Api")[[48]](#cite_note-GVP-Sangeang_Api-48) | Indonesia | 2014 | 15.2 |  |  | Ash drifted SE, grounding flights between south-east Asia and [Darwin, Australia](/wiki/Darwin,_Australia "Darwin, Australia").[[49]](#cite_note-49) |
| 4 | [Kelud](/wiki/Kelud "Kelud")[[50]](#cite_note-GVP-Kelud-50) | Indonesia | 2014 | 26 | 0.2–0.3[[51]](#cite_note-51) | 7 | Ash was ejected to an altitude exceeding 26 km. 7 people were killed and at least 100,000 people were evacuated. At least one commercial aircraft flew into the plume, later landing safely but incurring costly engine damage.[[52]](#cite_note-52) |
| 4 | [Mount Sinabung](/wiki/Mount_Sinabung "Mount Sinabung")[[53]](#cite_note-GVP-Sinabung-53) | Indonesia | 2013-2019 | 16.8 |  | 23 | [Mount Sinabung](/wiki/Mount_Sinabung "Mount Sinabung")'s eruptions caused many pyroclastic flows, one resulting in the loss of 16 lives.[[54]](#cite_note-:1-54) An eruption on 22 May 2016, resulted in the loss of 7 lives.[[55]](#cite_note-55) |
| 4 | [Nabro](/wiki/Nabro_Volcano "Nabro Volcano")[[56]](#cite_note-GVP-Nabro-56) | Eritrea | 2011 | 18 | 0.47[[57]](#cite_note-57) | 31[[58]](#cite_note-irin-58) | [2011 Nabro eruption](/wiki/2011_Nabro_eruption "2011 Nabro eruption") |

On 6 May, the eruption became more forceful and generated a wider and darker gray ash plume to an estimated altitude of 30,000 m (98,400 ft) into the stratosphere. From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
American annual computer science prize
.mw-parser-output .infobox-subbox{padding:0;border:none;margin:-3px;width:auto;min-width:100%;font-size:100%;clear:none;float:none;background-color:transparent;color:inherit}.mw-parser-output .infobox-3cols-child{margin:-3px}.mw-parser-output .infobox .navbar{font-size:100%}@media screen{html.skin-theme-clientpref-night .mw-parser-output .infobox-full-data:not(.notheme)>div:not(.notheme)[style]{background:#1f1f23!important;color:#f8f9fa}}@media screen and (prefers-color-scheme:dark){html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .infobox-full-data:not(.notheme)>div:not(.notheme)[style]{background:#1f1f23!important;color:#f8f9fa}}@media(min-width:640px){body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table{display:table!important}body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table>caption{display:table-caption!important}body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table>tbody{display:table-row-group}body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table th,body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table td{padding-left:inherit;padding-right:inherit}}
| ACM Turing Award | |
| --- | --- |
| [Statue of Alan Turing](/wiki/File:Turing_statue_Surrey.jpg) Statue of [Alan Turing](/wiki/Alan_Turing "Alan Turing") | |
| Awarded for | Outstanding contributions in [computer science](/wiki/Computer_science "Computer science") |
| Country | United States |
| Presented by | [Association for Computing Machinery](/wiki/Association_for_Computing_Machinery "Association for Computing Machinery") |
| Reward | US$1,000,000[[1]](#cite_note-million-1) |

| Website | [amturing.acm.org](https://amturing.acm.org) |
The **ACM A. M. Turing Award** is an annual prize given by the [Association for Computing Machinery](/wiki/Association_for_Computing_Machinery "Association for Computing Machinery") (ACM) for contributions of lasting and major technical importance to [computer science](/wiki/Computer_science "Computer science"). It is generally recognized as the highest distinction in the field of computer science and is often referred to as the "[Nobel Prize of Computing](/wiki/List_of_awards_considered_the_highest_in_a_field "List of awards considered the highest in a field")".[[2]](#cite_note-ACM-2)[[3]](#cite_note-BartoSutton-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) As of 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Turing_Award&action=edit), 81 people have been awarded the prize, with the most recent recipients being [Charles H. Bennett](/wiki/Charles_H._Bennett_(physicist) "Charles H. Bennett (physicist)") and [Gilles Brassard](/wiki/Gilles_Brassard "Gilles Brassard"), who won in 2025.
The award is named after [Alan Turing](/wiki/Alan_Turing "Alan Turing"), also referred as "Father of Computer Science", who was a British mathematician and [reader](/wiki/Reader_(academic_rank) "Reader (academic rank)") in mathematics at the [University of Manchester](/wiki/University_of_Manchester "University of Manchester"). Turing is often credited as being the founder of [theoretical computer science](/wiki/Theoretical_computer_science "Theoretical computer science") and [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence"),[[7]](#cite_note-7) and a key contributor to the Allied [cryptanalysis](/wiki/Cryptanalysis "Cryptanalysis") of the [Enigma cipher](/wiki/Enigma_cipher "Enigma cipher") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[8]](#cite_note-bbc-copeland-8) From 2007 to 2013, the award was accompanied by a prize of US$250,000, with financial support provided by [Intel](/wiki/Intel "Intel") and [Google](/wiki/Google "Google").[[2]](#cite_note-ACM-2)[[9]](#cite_note-9) Since 2014, the award has been accompanied by a prize of US$1 million, with financial support provided by Google.[[1]](#cite_note-million-1)[[10]](#cite_note-10)
The first recipient, in 1966, was [Alan Perlis](/wiki/Alan_Perlis "Alan Perlis"). The youngest recipient was [Donald Knuth](/wiki/Donald_Knuth "Donald Knuth"), who won in 1974 at the age of 36,[[11]](#cite_note-11) while the oldest recipient was [Alfred Aho](/wiki/Alfred_Aho "Alfred Aho"), who won in 2020 at the age of 79.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Three women have been awarded the prize: [Frances Allen](/wiki/Frances_Allen "Frances Allen") (in 2006),[[13]](#cite_note-Allen-13) [Barbara Liskov](/wiki/Barbara_Liskov "Barbara Liskov") (in 2008),[[14]](#cite_note-liskov-14) and [Shafi Goldwasser](/wiki/Shafi_Goldwasser "Shafi Goldwasser") (in 2012).[[15]](#cite_note-goldwasser-15)
[![Photo of The Turing Award on display at Nokia Bell Labs, August 2025.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/e/e3/Turing_Award%2C_on_display_at_Nokia_Bell_Labs%2C_NJ%2C_USA%2C_Aug_2025.jpg/250px-Turing_Award%2C_on_display_at_Nokia_Bell_Labs%2C_NJ%2C_USA%2C_Aug_2025.jpg)](/wiki/File:Turing_Award,_on_display_at_Nokia_Bell_Labs,_NJ,_USA,_Aug_2025.jpg)
The Turing Award of 1983, given to [Dennis Ritchie](/wiki/Dennis_Ritchie "Dennis Ritchie") and [Ken Thompson](/wiki/Ken_Thompson "Ken Thompson"), on display at [Nokia Bell Labs](/wiki/Nokia_Bell_Labs "Nokia Bell Labs").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/9/9e/1986_Jeno%27s_Pizza_-_15_-_Willie_Davis_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:1986_Jeno%27s_Pizza_-_15_-_Willie_Davis_(cropped).jpg)
The [Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers") defeated the [Chiefs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Chiefs "Kansas City Chiefs") in the first [AFL–NFL World Championship Game](/wiki/AFL%E2%80%93NFL_World_Championship_Game "AFL–NFL World Championship Game") ([Super Bowl I](/wiki/Super_Bowl_I "Super Bowl I")).
The [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") is the annual [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football") game that determines the champion of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL). The game culminates a [season](/wiki/National_Football_League_regular_season "National Football League regular season") that begins in the previous calendar year, and is the conclusion of the [NFL playoffs](/wiki/National_Football_League_playoffs "National Football League playoffs"). The winner receives the [Vince Lombardi Trophy](/wiki/Vince_Lombardi_Trophy "Vince Lombardi Trophy"). The contest is held in an American city, chosen three to four years beforehand,[[1]](#cite_note-1) usually at warm-weather sites or [domed stadiums](/wiki/Stadium#Types "Stadium").[[2]](#cite_note-2) Since January 1971, the winner of the [American Football Conference (AFC) Championship Game](/wiki/AFC_Championship_Game "AFC Championship Game") has faced the winner of the [National Football Conference (NFC) Championship Game](/wiki/NFC_Championship_Game "NFC Championship Game") in the culmination of the NFL playoffs.
Before the [1970 merger](/wiki/AFL%E2%80%93NFL_merger "AFL–NFL merger") between the [American Football League](/wiki/American_Football_League "American Football League") (AFL) and the NFL, the two leagues met in four such contests. The first two were marketed as the "AFL–NFL World Championship Game", but were also casually referred to as "the Super Bowl game" during the television broadcast.[[3]](#cite_note-3) [Super Bowl III](/wiki/Super_Bowl_III "Super Bowl III") in January 1969 was the first such game that carried the "Super Bowl" moniker in official marketing; the names "[Super Bowl I](/wiki/Super_Bowl_I "Super Bowl I")" and "[Super Bowl II](/wiki/Super_Bowl_II "Super Bowl II")" were retroactively applied to the first two games.[[4]](#cite_note-nflaflwcg-4)
A total of 20 franchises, including teams that have relocated to another city or changed their name, have won the Super Bowl.[[5]](#cite_note-record-5) There are four [NFL teams](/wiki/NFL_team "NFL team") that have never appeared in a Super Bowl: the [Cleveland Browns](/wiki/Cleveland_Browns "Cleveland Browns"), [Detroit Lions](/wiki/Detroit_Lions "Detroit Lions"), [Jacksonville Jaguars](/wiki/Jacksonville_Jaguars "Jacksonville Jaguars"), and [Houston Texans](/wiki/Houston_Texans "Houston Texans"), though both the Browns ([1950](/wiki/1950_NFL_Championship_Game "1950 NFL Championship Game"), [1954](/wiki/1954_NFL_Championship_Game "1954 NFL Championship Game"), [1955](/wiki/1955_NFL_Championship_Game "1955 NFL Championship Game"), [1964](/wiki/1964_NFL_Championship_Game "1964 NFL Championship Game")) and Lions ([1935](/wiki/1935_NFL_Championship_Game "1935 NFL Championship Game"), [1952](/wiki/1952_NFL_Championship_Game "1952 NFL Championship Game"), [1953](/wiki/1953_NFL_Championship_Game "1953 NFL Championship Game"), [1957](/wiki/1957_NFL_Championship_Game "1957 NFL Championship Game")) had won [NFL Championship Games](/wiki/NFL_Championship_Game "NFL Championship Game") prior to the creation of the Super Bowl in the [1966 season](/wiki/1966_NFL_season "1966 NFL season").
The [1972 Miami Dolphins](/wiki/1972_Miami_Dolphins_season "1972 Miami Dolphins season") capped off the only [perfect season](/wiki/Perfect_season "Perfect season") in [NFL history](/wiki/History_of_the_National_Football_League "History of the National Football League") with their victory in [Super Bowl VII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_VII "Super Bowl VII"). Only two franchises have ever won the Super Bowl while hosting at their home stadiums: the [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers "Tampa Bay Buccaneers") in [Super Bowl LV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LV "Super Bowl LV") and the [Los Angeles Rams](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Rams "Los Angeles Rams") in [Super Bowl LVI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LVI "Super Bowl LVI"). The reigning champions are the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/Seattle_Seahawks "Seattle Seahawks") following their victory over the [New England Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots") in [Super Bowl LX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LX "Super Bowl LX").
