Results
-------
Numbers in parentheses in the table are Super Bowl appearances as of the date of that Super Bowl and are used as follows:
* **Winning team** and **Losing team** columns indicate the number of times that team has appeared in a Super Bowl as well as each respective teams' Super Bowl record to date.
* **Venue** column indicates number of times that stadium has hosted a Super Bowl.
* **City** column indicates number of times that metropolitan area has hosted a Super Bowl.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
.mw-parser-output .tmulti .multiimageinner{display:flex;flex-direction:column}.mw-parser-output .tmulti .trow{display:flex;flex-direction:row;clear:left;flex-wrap:wrap;width:100%;box-sizing:border-box}.mw-parser-output .tmulti .tsingle{margin:1px;float:left}.mw-parser-output .tmulti .theader{clear:both;font-weight:bold;text-align:center;align-self:center;background-color:transparent;width:100%}.mw-parser-output .tmulti .thumbcaption{background-color:transparent}.mw-parser-output .tmulti .text-align-left{text-align:left}.mw-parser-output .tmulti .text-align-right{text-align:right}.mw-parser-output .tmulti .text-align-center{text-align:center}@media all and (max-width:720px){.mw-parser-output .tmulti .thumbinner{width:100%!important;box-sizing:border-box;max-width:none!important;align-items:center}.mw-parser-output .tmulti .trow{justify-content:center}.mw-parser-output .tmulti .tsingle{float:none!important;max-width:100%!important;box-sizing:border-box;text-align:center}.mw-parser-output .tmulti .tsingle .thumbcaption{text-align:left}.mw-parser-output .tmulti .trow>.thumbcaption{text-align:center}}@media screen{.mw-parser-output .tmulti .multiimageinner .thumbimage{background-color:inherit}html.skin-theme-clientpref-night .mw-parser-output .tmulti .multiimageinner .thumbimage:not([style\*="background-color"]) span:not(.skin-invert-image):not(.skin-invert):not(.bg-transparent) img,html.skin-theme-clientpref-day .mw-parser-output .tmulti .multiimageinner .thumbimage:not([style\*="background-color"]) span:not(.bg-transparent) img{background-color:white}}@media screen and (prefers-color-scheme:dark){html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .tmulti .multiimageinner .thumbimage:not([style\*="background-color"]) span:not(.skin-invert-image):not(.skin-invert):not(.bg-transparent) img{background-color:white}}
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/StephenGroverCleveland.jpg/250px-StephenGroverCleveland.jpg)](/wiki/File:StephenGroverCleveland.jpg)
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Official_Presidential_Portrait_of_President_Donald_J._Trump_%282025%29.jpg/250px-Official_Presidential_Portrait_of_President_Donald_J._Trump_%282025%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Official_Presidential_Portrait_of_President_Donald_J._Trump_(2025).jpg)
[Grover Cleveland](/wiki/Grover_Cleveland "Grover Cleveland") and [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") are the only two presidents to serve non-consecutive terms.
This is a **list of former [presidents of the United States](/wiki/Presidents_of_the_United_States "Presidents of the United States") who ran for office** (the [presidency](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"), a seat in [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress"), or [governor](/wiki/Governor_(United_States) "Governor (United States)")) after leaving office as president. It does not include presidents who sought reelection to a consecutive term while still in office. Prior to the passage of the [Twenty-second Amendment](/wiki/Twenty-second_Amendment "Twenty-second Amendment"), presidents could run for reelection without restriction;[[1]](#cite_note-1) since then, presidents can be reelected only once. Grover Cleveland was the first president to win reelection after leaving office.
Some presidents have been recruited, requested, or drafted to run again. This list, however, only includes those presidents who actively campaigned.
Presidency
----------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_former_presidents_of_the_United_States_who_ran_for_office&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Presidency")]
This list only includes *former* presidents who ran again for president.
| President | Previous term | Reason the president first left office | Year of attempted comeback | Result | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Martin Van Buren](/wiki/Martin_Van_Buren "Martin Van Buren")[[2]](#cite_note-2) | 1837–1841 | Defeated in the [general election](/wiki/1840_United_States_presidential_election "1840 United States presidential election") | [1844](/wiki/1844_United_States_presidential_election "1844 United States presidential election") | Failed nomination | Failed in his attempt to win the nomination of the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") |
| [1848](/wiki/1848_United_States_presidential_election "1848 United States presidential election") | Lost | First nominee of the newly formed [Free Soil Party](/wiki/Free_Soil_Party "Free Soil Party") |
| [Millard Fillmore](/wiki/Millard_Fillmore "Millard Fillmore")[[3]](#cite_note-3) | 1850–1853 | Denied nomination by his party | [1856](/wiki/1856_United_States_presidential_election "1856 United States presidential election") | Lost | Nominee for the [American Party (Know Nothing)](/wiki/Know_Nothing "Know Nothing") |
| [Ulysses S. Grant](/wiki/Ulysses_S._Grant "Ulysses S. Grant")[[4]](#cite_note-4) | 1869–1877 | Retired | [1880](/wiki/1880_United_States_presidential_election "1880 United States presidential election") | Failed nomination | Failed in his attempt to win the nomination of the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") |
| [Grover Cleveland](/wiki/Grover_Cleveland "Grover Cleveland")[[5]](#cite_note-5) | 1885–1889 | Defeated in the [general election](/wiki/1888_United_States_presidential_election "1888 United States presidential election") | [1892](/wiki/1892_United_States_presidential_election "1892 United States presidential election") | **Won** | First president to win a second nonconsecutive term (1893–1897) |
| [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt")[[6]](#cite_note-6) | 1901–1909 | Retired | [1912](/wiki/1912_United_States_presidential_election "1912 United States presidential election") | Lost | Nominee of the [Progressive Party (Bull Moose)](/wiki/Bull_Moose_Party "Bull Moose Party"), after he was denied the nomination of the Republican Party. |
| [Herbert Hoover](/wiki/Herbert_Hoover "Herbert Hoover")[[7]](#cite_note-7) | 1929–1933 | Defeated in the [general election](/wiki/1932_United_States_presidential_election "1932 United States presidential election") | [1940](/wiki/1940_United_States_presidential_election "1940 United States presidential election") | Failed nomination | Failed in his attempt to win the nomination of the Republican Party |
| [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump")[[8]](#cite_note-8) | 2017–2021 | Defeated in the [general election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election") | [2024](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election") | **Won** | Second president to win a second nonconsecutive term (2025–present) |
Other selected office
---------------------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_former_presidents_of_the_United_States_who_ran_for_office&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Other selected office")]
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/William_Howard_Taft_Portrait_%283x4_cropped%29.jpg/250px-William_Howard_Taft_Portrait_%283x4_cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_Howard_Taft_Portrait_(3x4_cropped).jpg)
[William Howard Taft](/wiki/William_Howard_Taft "William Howard Taft") is the only former president to serve as [chief justice of the United States](/wiki/Chief_justice_of_the_United_States "Chief justice of the United States").
| President | Presidential term | Reason for leaving office | Year of election | Office | Result | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [James Madison](/wiki/James_Madison "James Madison") | 1809–1817 | Chose not to run again | 1829 | [Virginia Constitutional Convention of 1829–1830](/wiki/Virginia_Constitutional_Convention_of_1829%E2%80%931830 "Virginia Constitutional Convention of 1829–1830") | **Won** | Represented [Orange County, Virginia](/wiki/Orange_County,_Virginia "Orange County, Virginia")[[9]](#cite_note-VA-9) |
| [James Monroe](/wiki/James_Monroe "James Monroe") | 1817–1825 | Chose not to run again | 1829 | Virginia Constitutional Convention of 1829–1830 | **Won** | Represented [Loudoun County, Virginia](/wiki/Loudoun_County,_Virginia "Loudoun County, Virginia")[[9]](#cite_note-VA-9) |
| Presiding officer of the Virginia Constitutional Convention of 1829–1830 | **Won** | Withdrew on December 8 due to failing health[[9]](#cite_note-VA-9) |
| [John Quincy Adams](/wiki/John_Quincy_Adams "John Quincy Adams") | 1825–1829 | Defeated in the [general election](/wiki/1828_United_States_presidential_election "1828 United States presidential election")[[10]](#cite_note-10) | 1830–1846 (9 elections) | [U.S. House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") | **Won** | Only former president to serve in the House, served until his 1848 death |
| [1833](/wiki/1833%E2%80%9334_Massachusetts_gubernatorial_election "1833–34 Massachusetts gubernatorial election") | [Governor of Massachusetts](/wiki/Governor_of_Massachusetts "Governor of Massachusetts") | Lost[[11]](#cite_note-11) | Continued in House after defeat |
| [John Tyler](/wiki/John_Tyler "John Tyler") | 1841–1845 | Denied nomination by his party/withdrew from race | 1861 | [Confederate States Congress](/wiki/Confederate_States_Congress "Confederate States Congress") | **Won** | Died before he could take office (had served in the unelected [Provisional Congress](/wiki/Provisional_Congress_of_the_Confederate_States "Provisional Congress of the Confederate States")).[[12]](#cite_note-12) Only former president to ever run for an office outside the United States |
| [Andrew Johnson](/wiki/Andrew_Johnson "Andrew Johnson") | 1865–1869 | Denied nomination by his party | 1872 | U.S. House of Representatives | Lost | Ran as an Independent and finished 3rd in the general election[[13]](#cite_note-The_Presidency_of_Andrew_Johnson-13) |
| 1874 | [U.S. Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") | **Won** | Only former president to serve in the Senate, served until his 1875 death[[13]](#cite_note-The_Presidency_of_Andrew_Johnson-13) |
| [William Howard Taft](/wiki/William_Howard_Taft "William Howard Taft") | 1909–1913 | Defeated in the [general election](/wiki/1912_United_States_presidential_election "1912 United States presidential election") | 1921[[14]](#cite_note-14) | [Chief Justice of the United States](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_the_United_States "Chief Justice of the United States") | **Confirmed** | Only former president ever to serve on the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States"), served until his 1930 resignation[[15]](#cite_note-15) |
See also

References

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/TheSmiler2015%282%29.JPG/250px-TheSmiler2015%282%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:TheSmiler2015(2).JPG)
[The Smiler](/wiki/The_Smiler "The Smiler") is the roller coaster with the most [inversions](/wiki/Roller_coaster_inversion "Roller coaster inversion") in the world.
[Roller coasters](/wiki/Roller_coaster "Roller coaster") are [amusement rides](/wiki/Amusement_ride "Amusement ride") developed for [amusement parks](/wiki/Amusement_park "Amusement park") and modern theme parks. Early iterations during the 16th and 17th centuries, popularized in Russia, involved wooden sleds that carried riders down large slides made from ice. The first roller coasters to attach a train to a wooden track appeared in France in the early 1800s.[[1]](#cite_note-1800s_roller_coasters-1) Although [wooden roller coasters](/wiki/Wooden_roller_coaster "Wooden roller coaster") are still being produced, [steel roller coasters](/wiki/Steel_roller_coaster "Steel roller coaster") were introduced in the mid-20th-century and eventually became more common. They can be found on every continent except [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica").[[2]](#cite_note-Census-2)
Amusement parks often compete to build the tallest, fastest, and longest rides to attract thrill seekers and boost park attendance.[[3]](#cite_note-Amusement_park_competition-3) Ranked by height, speed, length, and number of [inversions](/wiki/Roller_coaster_inversion "Roller coaster inversion"), roller coasters often became the focal point for competing parks. Computer-simulated models led to innovations that produced more intense thrills while improving quality and durability.[[4]](#cite_note-Coaster_Wars-4) The debut of [Magnum XL-200](/wiki/Magnum_XL-200 "Magnum XL-200") in 1989 at [Cedar Point](/wiki/Cedar_Point "Cedar Point") introduced the first complete-circuit roller coaster to exceed 200 feet (61 m) in height,[[5]](#cite_note-CP_opening_weekend-5)[[6]](#cite_note-20th_anniversary_video-6) marking a pivot point in the industry and a new era sometimes referred to as the Coaster Wars.[[4]](#cite_note-Coaster_Wars-4) This period saw increasing competition as parks sought to be the latest to set a new world record, and some records were held for less than a year.[[7]](#cite_note-Coaster_War_Resurgence-7)
The pace of competition eventually slowed, however.[[4]](#cite_note-Coaster_Wars-4)[[7]](#cite_note-Coaster_War_Resurgence-7) The now-defunct [Kingda Ka](/wiki/Kingda_Ka "Kingda Ka"), previously the tallest coaster in the world at 456 feet (139 m),[[8]](#cite_note-Kingda_Ka-8) held the height record from 2005 until its closure in 2024.[[4]](#cite_note-Coaster_Wars-4)[[7]](#cite_note-Coaster_War_Resurgence-7) [Formula Rossa](/wiki/Formula_Rossa "Formula Rossa"), formerly the world's fastest at 149 mph (240 km/h), held the speed record for 15 years.[[9]](#cite_note-Formula_Rossa-9) [Steel Dragon 2000](/wiki/Steel_Dragon_2000 "Steel Dragon 2000"), with its track length of 8,133 feet (2,479 m), was the longest in the world for more than 25 years. These records were eventually surpassed by [Falcons Flight](/wiki/Falcons_Flight "Falcons Flight") in 2025, which opened as the tallest, fastest, and longest roller coaster in the world. Other notables include [The Beast](/wiki/The_Beast_(roller_coaster) "The Beast (roller coaster)"), the world's longest wooden coaster since its opening in 1979, featuring a track length of 7,361 feet (2,244 m),[[10]](#cite_note-Beast-RCDB-10) and [The Smiler](/wiki/The_Smiler "The Smiler"), which set a world record with fourteen inversions in 2013.[[11]](#cite_note-Smiler-11)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Viral disease affecting humans
.mw-parser-output .hatnote{font-style:italic}.mw-parser-output div.hatnote{padding-left:1.6em;margin-bottom:0.5em}.mw-parser-output .hatnote i{font-style:normal}.mw-parser-output .hatnote+link+.hatnote{margin-top:-0.5em}@media print{body.ns-0 .mw-parser-output .hatnote{display:none!important}}
This article is about the disease. For other uses, see [Measles (disambiguation)](/wiki/Measles_(disambiguation) "Measles (disambiguation)").
Not to be confused with either [rubella](/wiki/Rubella "Rubella") (sometimes called "German measles") or [roseola](/wiki/Roseola "Roseola"), other viral diseases that cause a rash and fever.
Medical condition

| [Specialty](/wiki/Medical_specialty "Medical specialty") | [Infectious disease](/wiki/Infectious_disease_(medical_specialty) "Infectious disease (medical specialty)") |
| [Symptoms](/wiki/Signs_and_symptoms "Signs and symptoms") | Fever, cough, runny nose, red eyes, rash[[3]](#cite_note-Tesini-2023-3)[[4]](#cite_note-CDC_Symptoms-2014-4) |
| [Complications](/wiki/Complication_(medicine) "Complication (medicine)") | [Pneumonia](/wiki/Pneumonia "Pneumonia"), [seizures](/wiki/Seizure "Seizure"), [encephalitis](/wiki/Encephalitis "Encephalitis"), [subacute sclerosing panencephalitis](/wiki/Subacute_sclerosing_panencephalitis "Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis"), [immunosuppression](/wiki/Immunosuppression "Immunosuppression"), hearing loss, blindness[[5]](#cite_note-CDC_Pink_Book-2024-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Rota-2016-6) |
| Usual onset | 10–12 days after exposure[[7]](#cite_note-WHO_Facts-2024a-7)[[8]](#cite_note-Bope-2014-8) |
| Duration | 7–10 days[[7]](#cite_note-WHO_Facts-2024a-7)[[8]](#cite_note-Bope-2014-8) (not including complications) |
| [Causes](/wiki/Cause_(medicine) "Cause (medicine)") | [Measles virus](/wiki/Measles_virus "Measles virus")[[3]](#cite_note-Tesini-2023-3) |
| Prevention | [Measles vaccine](/wiki/Measles_vaccine "Measles vaccine")[[7]](#cite_note-WHO_Facts-2024a-7) |
| Treatment | [Supportive care](/wiki/Supportive_treatment "Supportive treatment")[[7]](#cite_note-WHO_Facts-2024a-7) |
| Frequency | 10 million per year[[3]](#cite_note-Tesini-2023-3) |
| Deaths | 140,000+ (2018)[[9]](#cite_note-More_than_140-2019-9)[[10]](#cite_note-CDC_Global-2020-10) |

**Measles** (probably from [Middle Dutch](/wiki/Middle_Dutch "Middle Dutch") or [Middle High German](/wiki/Middle_High_German "Middle High German") *masel(e)*, meaning "blemish, [blood blister](/wiki/Blood_blister "Blood blister")")[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) is a highly contagious, [vaccine-preventable](/wiki/Vaccine-preventable_diseases "Vaccine-preventable diseases") [infectious disease](/wiki/Infectious_disease "Infectious disease") caused by [measles virus](/wiki/Measles_morbillivirus "Measles morbillivirus").[[3]](#cite_note-Tesini-2023-3)[[5]](#cite_note-CDC_Pink_Book-2024-5) Other names include *morbilli*, *rubeola*, *9-day measles, red measles*, and *English measles*.[[1]](#cite_note-Milner-2015-1)[[3]](#cite_note-Tesini-2023-3)
Symptoms usually develop 10–12 days after exposure to an infected person and last 7–10 days.[[7]](#cite_note-WHO_Facts-2024a-7) Initial symptoms typically include [fever](/wiki/Fever "Fever"), often greater than 40 °C (104 °F), cough, [runny nose](/wiki/Rhinitis "Rhinitis"), and [inflamed eyes](/wiki/Conjunctivitis "Conjunctivitis").[[3]](#cite_note-Tesini-2023-3)[[4]](#cite_note-CDC_Symptoms-2014-4) Small white spots known as [Koplik spots](/wiki/Koplik%27s_spots "Koplik's spots") may form inside the mouth two or three days after the start of symptoms.[[4]](#cite_note-CDC_Symptoms-2014-4) A red, flat rash which usually starts on the face and then spreads to the rest of the body typically begins three to five days after the start of symptoms.[[4]](#cite_note-CDC_Symptoms-2014-4) Common complications include [diarrhea](/wiki/Diarrhea "Diarrhea") (in 8% of cases), [middle ear infection](/wiki/Otitis_media "Otitis media") (7%), and [pneumonia](/wiki/Pneumonia "Pneumonia") (6%).[[5]](#cite_note-CDC_Pink_Book-2024-5) These occur in part due to measles-induced [immunosuppression](/wiki/Immunosuppression "Immunosuppression").[[6]](#cite_note-Rota-2016-6) Less commonly, [seizures](/wiki/Seizure "Seizure"), [blindness](/wiki/Blindness "B

question: As of now, which country or countries have won a single medal in the history of the Summer Olympic Games—and it was a gold?

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Lin_Dan.jpg/250px-Lin_Dan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lin_Dan.jpg)
[Lin Dan](/wiki/Lin_Dan "Lin Dan") is the first player in mens single to win consecutive golds in the [2008](/wiki/2008_Summer_Olympics "2008 Summer Olympics") and [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics").
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Badminton-gao_ling_crop.jpg/250px-Badminton-gao_ling_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Badminton-gao_ling_crop.jpg)
[Gao Ling](/wiki/Gao_Ling "Gao Ling") won a total of 4 olympic medals in badminton, with 2 being consecutive mixed doubles gold
[Badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton") is a sport contested at the [Summer Olympic Games](/wiki/Summer_Olympic_Games "Summer Olympic Games"). Badminton was first held as a [demonstration sport](/wiki/Demonstration_sport "Demonstration sport") at the [1972 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1972_Summer_Olympics "1972 Summer Olympics"), and was an exhibition sport at the [1988 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1988_Summer_Olympics "1988 Summer Olympics"); the men's and women's singles and doubles have been held at every Summer Olympics since the [1992 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1992_Summer_Olympics "1992 Summer Olympics").[[1]](#cite_note-1) The mixed doubles badminton tournament started in the [1996 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1996_Summer_Olympics "1996 Summer Olympics").[[2]](#cite_note-BWF-2)
The [Badminton World Federation](/wiki/Badminton_World_Federation "Badminton World Federation") (BWF) rankings are used to determine the qualification of the players for the tournament. Nations can enter a maximum of two players each in the men's and women's singles if both are ranked in the world's top 16; otherwise, one quota place until the roster of thirty-eight players has been completed. Similar regulations also apply to the players competing in the doubles, as the NOCs (National Olympic Committees) can enter a maximum of two pairs if both are ranked in the top eight, while the remaining NOCs are entitled to one until the quota of 16 highest-ranked pairs is filled. The host nation, if it has not already qualified two competitors, receives at least either two singles players or one pair.[[3]](#cite_note-Qual-3)
[Gao Ling](/wiki/Gao_Ling "Gao Ling") is the all-time leader for the most Olympic medals in badminton, with two golds, one silver, and one bronze; [Fu Haifeng](/wiki/Fu_Haifeng "Fu Haifeng") (two golds, one silver), [Zhang Nan](/wiki/Zhang_Nan_(badminton) "Zhang Nan (badminton)"), [Zhao Yunlei](/wiki/Zhao_Yunlei "Zhao Yunlei"), [Viktor Axelsen](/wiki/Viktor_Axelsen "Viktor Axelsen") and [Kim Dong-moon](/wiki/Kim_Dong-moon "Kim Dong-moon") (two golds, one bronze) each, [Gil Young-ah](/wiki/Gil_Young-ah "Gil Young-ah") and [Chen Long](/wiki/Chen_Long "Chen Long") (one each) and [Lee Chong Wei](/wiki/Lee_Chong_Wei "Lee Chong Wei") (three silvers) are second for the most medals in badminton, each with three. [Fu Haifeng](/wiki/Fu_Haifeng "Fu Haifeng"), [Gao Ling](/wiki/Gao_Ling "Gao Ling"), [Ge Fei](/wiki/Ge_Fei_(badminton) "Ge Fei (badminton)"), [Gu Jun](/wiki/Gu_Jun "Gu Jun"), [Kim Dong-moon](/wiki/Kim_Dong-moon "Kim Dong-moon"), [Lee Yang](/wiki/Lee_Yang "Lee Yang"), [Lin Dan](/wiki/Lin_Dan "Lin Dan"), [Viktor Axelsen](/wiki/Viktor_Axelsen "Viktor Axelsen"), [Wang Chi Lin](/wiki/Wang_Chi-Lin "Wang Chi-Lin"), [Zhang Jun](/wiki/Zhang_Jun_(badminton) "Zhang Jun (badminton)"), [Zhang Nan](/wiki/Zhang_Nan_(badminton) "Zhang Nan (badminton)"), [Zhang Ning](/wiki/Zhang_Ning "Zhang Ning") and [Zhao Yunlei](/wiki/Zhao_Yunlei "Zhao Yunlei") are the all-time leaders for the most gold medal wins, with two.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In the 1992 Summer Olympics, future married couple [Susi Susanti](/wiki/Susi_Susanti "Susi Susanti") and [Alan Budikusuma](/wiki/Alan_Budikusuma "Alan Budikusuma") won Indonesia their first ever Olympic gold medals since their first Olympic participation in [1952](/wiki/1952_Summer_Olympics "1952 Summer Olympics"), while brothers [Jalani](/wiki/Jalani_Sidek "Jalani Sidek") and [Razif Sidek](/wiki/Razif_Sidek "Razif Sidek") were the first Malaysian Olympic medalists since Malaysia first participated in the [1964 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1964_Summer_Olympics "1964 Summer Olympics").[[5]](#cite_note-5) [Mia Audina](/wiki/Mia_Audina "Mia Audina") won her first silver in the 1996 Olympics representing Indonesia, but won her second silver in the [2004 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2004_Summer_Olympics "2004 Summer Olympics") with the Netherlands, the only badminton medalist to ever win for two different countries.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In the [2000 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Badminton_at_the_2000_Summer_Olympics "Badminton at the 2000 Summer Olympics"), China swept the [women's doubles tournament](/wiki/Badminton_at_the_2000_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_doubles "Badminton at the 2000 Summer Olympics – Women's doubles"), winning all three medals, making it the only sweep in Olympic badminton history.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Indonesia also did this in the [1992 Olympics men's singles tournament](/wiki/Badminton_at_the_1992_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_singles "Badminton at the 1992 Summer Olympics – Men's singles"), but there was no bronze medal match in that Games so the medal was shared with Danish player [Thomas Stuer-Lauridsen](/wiki/Thomas_Stuer-Lauridsen "Thomas Stuer-Lauridsen"). In the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Badminton_at_the_2012_Summer_Olympics "Badminton at the 2012 Summer Olympics"), China became the first country to win all five disciplines' gold medal in history, and as of 2024 the only clean sweep in the same Games. Indonesia became the second to achieve this feat, stretching from its first gold in the [1992 women's singles](/wiki/Badminton_at_the_1992_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_singles "Badminton at the 1992 Summer Olympics – Women's singles") to [2020 women's doubles](/wiki/Badminton_at_the_2020_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_doubles "Badminton at the 2020 Summer Olympics – Women's doubles").[[8]](#cite_note-8)
As of the [2024 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Badminton_at_the_2024_Summer_Olympics "Badminton at the 2024 Summer Olympics"), China has been the most successful nation in badminton, winning 52 medals; 37 of them were from the women's singles and doubles and mixed doubles tournaments. Indonesia, South Korea (both 22 medals), and Malaysia (11) are the only other nations to have more than ten medals. As many as 126 medals (44 gold, 44 silver, and 48 bronze) have been awarded to 168 medalists from 13 NOCs.[[9]](#cite_note-9) There were four additional bronze medal winners in the 1992 Summer Olympics because no bronze medal matches were played in any of the four tournaments.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
.mw-parser-output .hatnote{font-style:italic}.mw-parser-output div.hatnote{padding-left:1.6em;margin-bottom:0.5em}.mw-parser-output .hatnote i{font-style:normal}.mw-parser-output .hatnote+link+.hatnote{margin-top:-0.5em}@media print{body.ns-0 .mw-parser-output .hatnote{display:none!important}}
Main article: [List of world records in swimming](/wiki/List_of_world_records_in_swimming "List of world records in swimming")
The first **world record in the men's 400 metres [freestyle](/wiki/Freestyle_swimming "Freestyle swimming")** in long course (50 metres) swimming was recognised by [FINA](/wiki/World_Aquatics "World Aquatics") (now World Aquatics) in 1908. In the short course (25 metres) swimming events the world's governing body recognizes world records since 3 March 1991.
The drop in world record times in 2008–2009 coincided with the introduction of polyurethane suits from Speedo ([LZR](/wiki/LZR_Racer "LZR Racer"), 50% [polyurethane](/wiki/Polyurethane "Polyurethane")) in 2008 and by Arena ([X-Glide](/wiki/X-Glide "X-Glide")), Adidas (Hydrofoil) and Italian swimming suit manufacturer Jaked (all 100% polyurethane) in 2009. FINA's ban on non-textile suit came into effect in January 2010.[[1]](#cite_note-1) FINA also released a list of approved suits.
Men
---

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
[![The theatrical poster for Ben-Hur (1925) depicting a man behaving in a sexually aggressive manner towards a woman cowering from him.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Ben-Hur-1925.jpg/250px-Ben-Hur-1925.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ben-Hur-1925.jpg)
*[Ben-Hur](/wiki/Ben-Hur:_A_Tale_of_the_Christ_(1925_film) "Ben-Hur: A Tale of the Christ (1925 film)")* (1925) was the most expensive film of the [silent era](/wiki/Silent_era "Silent era"), possibly holding the record for over twenty years.
It is not clear which film is the most expensive ever made, due to the secretive nature of [Hollywood accounting](/wiki/Hollywood_accounting "Hollywood accounting"). *[Star Wars: The Force Awakens](/wiki/Star_Wars:_The_Force_Awakens "Star Wars: The Force Awakens")* holds the official record with a net [budget](/wiki/Film_budgeting "Film budgeting") of $536 million. The production of the third and fourth *[Avengers](/wiki/List_of_Marvel_Cinematic_Universe_films "List of Marvel Cinematic Universe films")* films—*[Infinity War](/wiki/Avengers:_Infinity_War "Avengers: Infinity War")* and *[Endgame](/wiki/Avengers:_Endgame "Avengers: Endgame")*—stands as the most expensive [back-to-back film production](/wiki/Back-to-back_film_production "Back-to-back film production"), with a cost of over $1 billion.
Inflation, filming techniques, and external market forces affect the cost of film production. Costs rose steadily during the [silent era](/wiki/Silent_film "Silent film"); 1925's *[Ben-Hur: A Tale of the Christ](/wiki/Ben-Hur:_A_Tale_of_the_Christ_(1925_film) "Ben-Hur: A Tale of the Christ (1925 film)")* set a record that lasted well into the [sound era](/wiki/Sound_film "Sound film"). Television had an impact on rising costs in the 1950s and early 1960s as cinema competed with it for audiences; 1963's highest-earning film, *[Cleopatra](/wiki/Cleopatra_(1963_film) "Cleopatra (1963 film)"),* did not recoup its costs on its original release. The 1990s saw two thresholds crossed: 1994's *[True Lies](/wiki/True_Lies "True Lies")* cost $100 million and 1997's *[Titanic](/wiki/Titanic_(1997_film) "Titanic (1997 film)")* cost $200 million, both directed by [James Cameron](/wiki/James_Cameron "James Cameron"). The 21st century has so far seen the $300 million and $400 million thresholds crossed and it has become normal for a [tent-pole feature](/wiki/Tent-pole_programming "Tent-pole programming") from a [major film studio](/wiki/Major_film_studio "Major film studio") to cost over $200 million, and an increasing number of films now cost more than $300 million.
This list contains only films already released to the general public and not films that are still in production or post-production, as costs can change during the production process. Listed below is the net [negative cost](/wiki/Negative_cost "Negative cost"): the costs of the actual filming, not including promotional costs (i.e. advertisements, commercials, posters, etc.) and after accounting for tax subsidies. The charts are ordered by budgets that have been independently audited or officially acknowledged by the production companies where they are known; most companies will not give a statement on the actual production costs, so often only estimates by professional researchers and movie industry writers are available. Where budget estimates conflict, the productions are charted by lower-bound estimates.
Most expensive productions (unadjusted for inflation)
-----------------------------------------------------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_most_expensive_films&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Most expensive productions (unadjusted for inflation)")]
Productions with a net budget of over $200 million in [nominal](/wiki/Real_versus_nominal_value_(economics) "Real versus nominal value (economics)") [U.S. dollars](/wiki/U.S._dollar "U.S. dollar") are listed here. Due to [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation"), all of the films on the chart have been produced in the 21st century.

[![Extended-protected article](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/8/8c/Extended-protection-shackle.svg/20px-Extended-protection-shackle.svg.png)](/wiki/Wikipedia:Protection_policy#extended "This article is extended-confirmed-protected.")
From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
[![A screencap of the title card from the trailer of Gone with the Wind.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Poster_-_Gone_With_the_Wind_01.jpg/250px-Poster_-_Gone_With_the_Wind_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Poster_-_Gone_With_the_Wind_01.jpg)
*[Gone with the Wind](/wiki/Gone_with_the_Wind_(film) "Gone with the Wind (film)")* held the record for the highest-grossing film for twenty-five years and, when adjusted for inflation, has earned more than any other film
[Films](/wiki/Film "Film") generate income from several revenue streams, including [theatrical exhibition](/wiki/Movie_theater "Movie theater"), [home video](/wiki/Home_video "Home video"), [television broadcast rights](/wiki/Broadcasting_rights "Broadcasting rights"), and [merchandising](/wiki/Merchandising "Merchandising"). However, theatrical [box-office](/wiki/Box_office "Box office") earnings are the primary metric for trade publications in assessing the success of a film, mostly because of the availability of the data compared to sales figures for home video and broadcast rights, but also because of historical practice. Included on the list are charts of the top box-office earners (ranked by both the [nominal and real value](/wiki/Real_versus_nominal_value_(economics) "Real versus nominal value (economics)") of their revenue), a chart of high-grossing films by calendar year, a timeline showing the transition of the highest-grossing film record, and a chart of the highest-grossing film franchises and series. All charts are ranked by international theatrical box-office performance where possible, excluding income derived from home video, broadcasting rights, and merchandise.
Traditionally, [war films](/wiki/War_film "War film"), [musicals](/wiki/Musical_film "Musical film"), and [historical dramas](/wiki/List_of_historical_drama_films "List of historical drama films") have been the most popular genres, but [franchise films](/wiki/Film_franchise "Film franchise") have been among the best performers of the 21st century. There is strong interest in the [superhero genre](/wiki/Superhero_film "Superhero film"), with eleven films in the [Marvel Cinematic Universe](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe "Marvel Cinematic Universe") featuring among the nominal top-earners. The most successful superhero film, *[Avengers: Endgame](/wiki/Avengers:_Endgame "Avengers: Endgame")*, is also the second-highest-grossing film on the nominal earnings chart, and there are four films in total based on the *[Avengers](/wiki/Avengers_(comics) "Avengers (comics)")* comic books charting in the top twenty. Other [Marvel Comics adaptations](/wiki/List_of_films_based_on_Marvel_Comics_publications "List of films based on Marvel Comics publications") have also had success with the *[Spider-Man](/wiki/Spider-Man_in_film "Spider-Man in film")* and *[X-Men](/wiki/X-Men_(film_series) "X-Men (film series)")* properties, while films based on *[Batman](/wiki/Batman_in_film "Batman in film")* and *[Superman](/wiki/Superman_in_film "Superman in film")* from [DC Comics](/wiki/DC_Comics "DC Comics") have generally performed well. Beyond the superhero genre, *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* is the most represented franchise in the nominal earnings chart with five films. Although the nominal earnings chart is dominated by films adapted from pre-existing properties and sequels, it is headed by *[Avatar](/wiki/Avatar_(2009_film) "Avatar (2009 film)")*, which is an original work. Animated family films have performed consistently well, with [Disney films](/wiki/List_of_Disney_theatrical_animated_features "List of Disney theatrical animated features") enjoying lucrative re-releases prior to the home-video era. Disney also enjoyed later success with films such as *[Frozen](/wiki/Frozen_(2013_film) "Frozen (2013 film)")* and [its sequel](/wiki/Frozen_2 "Frozen 2"), *[Zootopia](/wiki/Zootopia "Zootopia")* and [its sequel](/wiki/Zootopia_2 "Zootopia 2"), and *[The Lion King](/wiki/The_Lion_King "The Lion King")* (along with [its computer-animated remake](/wiki/The_Lion_King_(2019_film) "The Lion King (2019 film)")), as well as its [Pixar](/wiki/List_of_Pixar_films "List of Pixar films") division, of which *[Inside Out 2](/wiki/Inside_Out_2 "Inside Out 2")*, *[Incredibles 2](/wiki/Incredibles_2 "Incredibles 2")*, and *[Toy Story 3](/wiki/Toy_Story_3 "Toy Story 3")* and *[4](/wiki/Toy_Story_4 "Toy Story 4")* have been the best performers. Beyond Disney and Pixar animation, China's *[Ne Zha 2](/wiki/Ne_Zha_2 "Ne Zha 2")* (the [highest-grossing animated film](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_animated_films "List of highest-grossing animated films")), and the *[Despicable Me](/wiki/Despicable_Me "Despicable Me")* and *[Shrek](/wiki/Shrek_(franchise) "Shrek (franchise)")* series have met with the most success.
While [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation") has eroded the achievements of most films from the 1960s and 1970s, there are franchises originating from that period that are still active. Besides the *Star Wars* franchise, *[James Bond](/wiki/James_Bond_in_film "James Bond in film")* films are still being released periodically; both are among the highest-grossing franchises. Some of the older films that held the record of highest-grossing film still have respectable grosses by today's standards, but no longer compete numerically against today's top-earners in an era of much higher individual ticket prices. When those prices are adjusted for inflation, however, then *[Gone with the Wind](/wiki/Gone_with_the_Wind_(film) "Gone with the Wind (film)")*—which was the highest-grossing film outright for twenty-five years—is still the highest-grossing film of all time. All grosses on the list are expressed in [U.S. dollars](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar") at their nominal value, except where stated otherwise.

Highest-grossing films
----------------------
[![A portrait of a middle aged man with greying hair.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/James_Cameron_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/250px-James_Cameron_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)](/wiki/File:James_Cameron_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)
Three of the four highest-grossing films, including *Avatar* at the top, were written and directed by [James Cameron](/wiki/James_Cameron "James Cameron").
With a worldwide box-office gross of over $2.9 billion, *Avatar* is proclaimed to be the "highest-grossing" film, but such claims usually refer to [theatrical](/wiki/Movie_theatre "Movie theatre") revenues only and do not take into account [home video](/wiki/Home_video "Home video") and [television](/wiki/Television "Television") income, which can form a significant portion of a film's earnings. From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
.mw-parser-output .hatnote{font-style:italic}.mw-parser-output div.hatnote{padding-left:1.6em;margin-bottom:0.5em}.mw-parser-output .hatnote i{font-style:normal}.mw-parser-output .hatnote+link+.hatnote{margin-top:-0.5em}@media print{body.ns-0 .mw-parser-output .hatnote{display:none!important}}
"Megamerger" redirects here. For distributed algorithm in computing, see [Mega-Merger](/wiki/Mega-Merger "Mega-Merger").
The following tables list the largest [mergers and acquisitions](/wiki/Mergers_and_acquisitions "Mergers and acquisitions") by decade of transaction. Transaction values are given in the [US dollar](/wiki/US_dollar "US dollar") value for the year of the merger, adjusted for inflation. As of February 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=List_of_largest_mergers_and_acquisitions&action=edit), the largest ever acquisition was the 2026 takeover of [xAI](/wiki/XAI_(company) "XAI (company)") by [SpaceX](/wiki/SpaceX "SpaceX") at $250 billion ($250 billion adjusted for inflation). [AT&T](/wiki/AT%26T "AT&T") appears in these lists the most times with six entries, for a combined transaction value of $334.4 billion. Mergers and acquisitions are notated with the year the transaction was initiated, not necessarily completed. Mergers are shown as the market value of the combined entities.
